topics.title: First (Sanger) and Second (Massively Parallel) Generations of Sequencing: Examples and Comparative Overview of Available Platforms (Illumina, Roche, Applied Biosystems) - Principles, Advantages, and Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT755 - NEXT GENERATION SEQUENCING TECHNOLOGIES: DATA ANALYSIS AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** First (Sanger) and Second (Massively Parallel) Generations of Sequencing: Examples and Comparative Overview of Available Platforms (Illumina, Roche, Applied Biosystems) - Principles, Advantages, and Challenges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**DNA Sequencing** is the laboratory process of determining the precise order of nucleotides — Adenine (A), Guanine (G), Cytosine (C), and Thymine (T) — within a DNA molecule. Sequencing technologies are historically classified into three generations based on their underlying biochemical principle and scalability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition Block**
> - **First Generation Sequencing (FGS)**: The Sanger chain-termination method (1977), a low-throughput, high-accuracy, single-template read method producing reads of 500–1000 bp.
> - **Second Generation Sequencing (SGS)** / **Massively Parallel Sequencing (MPS) / Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS)**: Platforms that amplify DNA fragments clonally (in vitro or in situ) and sequence millions of fragments in parallel, producing gigabases (Gb) to terabases (Tb) of data per run.
> - **Third Generation Sequencing (TGS)**: Single-molecule, real-time sequencing (SMRT, Nanopore) — *out of scope for this topic*.

### 1.2 The Three Platforms of Focus (Second Generation)

| Platform | Manufacturer | Underlying Chemistry | Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Illumina** | Illumina (Solexa) | Sequencing-By-Synthesis (SBS) with reversible terminators | **Industry gold standard** |
| **Roche 454** | Roche Diagnostics | Pyrosequencing (luciferase-based chemiluminescence) | **Discontinued (2016)** |
| **Applied Biosystems SOLiD** | Life Technologies (Thermo Fisher) | Sequencing-By-Ligation (SBL) with 2-base encoding | **Discontinued (2017)** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Library Analogy — Reading a Million-Page Book**
> - **Sanger (1st Gen)** is like a single scholar sitting at a desk, reading one page of a million-page book at a time and writing down every letter. Extremely accurate, but painfully slow.
> - **Illumina (2nd Gen)** is like a modern printing press that first **photocopies every page into millions of identical fragments** (cluster amplification), then hires **millions of cameras** to snap fluorescent photographs of each fragment one letter at a time, all happening **simultaneously in a flow cell**.
> - **Roche 454 (2nd Gen)** is like the same photocopying trick, but instead of a camera, the lab uses a **glow-in-the-dark chemical reaction** — every time a new letter is added, a tiny flash of light is emitted, and a sensor counts the flash.
> - **SOLiD (2nd Gen)** is like reading a book **two letters at a time** with a clever color-coding system that checks each letter **twice** for extra accuracy.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module

- **Phred Quality Score (Q-score)**: A logarithmic measure of base-calling accuracy.
- **Read Length**: Number of contiguous bases produced per read (in bp).
- **Throughput**: Total bases generated per run (in Mb / Gb / Tb).
- **Coverage (Depth)**: Average number of times each nucleotide in the reference genome is represented in the sequencing data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sanger Sequencing — The Chain-Termination Principle

**Why it works:** The incorporation of a **2′,3′-dideoxynucleoside triphosphate (ddNTP)** permanently halts DNA strand elongation because the sugar lacks a free 3′-hydroxyl group required for the next phosphodiester bond.

**Operational Steps (Logic Bullets):**

1. **Template preparation**: A single-stranded DNA template is hybridized with a primer.
2. **Four parallel reactions** (historically) — each tube contains:
   - DNA polymerase
   - All four normal dNTPs (dATP, dTTP, dGTP, dCTP)
   - A small amount of **one** labeled ddNTP (e.g., ddATP)
3. **Random termination**: As polymerase extends the primer, ddNTPs compete with dNTPs. Random incorporation of a ddNTP terminates that particular strand.
4. **Fragment generation**: The result is a population of strands of **every possible length**, each ending at a specific base type.
5. **Separation**: Capillary electrophoresis separates fragments by size (single-base resolution).
6. **Detection**: Fluorescent labels are excited by a laser; the emission wavelength identifies the terminating base.
7. **Base-calling**: Software reconstructs the sequence from the ordered fragment lengths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Sanger Upgrade — Dye-Terminator Sequencing**: A single tube now contains all four ddNTPs, each labeled with a **different fluorescent dye**. This is what modern capillary instruments (e.g., Applied Biosystems 3730xl) use — no need for four separate reactions.

### 2.2 Illumina Sequencing — Sequencing-By-Synthesis (SBS)

**Why it works:** Each cycle, a DNA polymerase incorporates one fluorescently labeled reversible terminator (RT) nucleotide. The RT has a **chemically blocked 3′-OH** that prevents further extension. After imaging, the block and the dye are chemically cleaved, allowing the next cycle to proceed.

**Operational Steps (Logic Bullets):**

1. **Library preparation**: Genomic DNA is fragmented (typically 200–600 bp), adapters are ligated to both ends.
2. **Cluster generation (Bridge Amplification)** on a glass flow cell:
   - The flow-cell surface is coated with **oligonucleotides complementary to the adapters**.
   - Fragments bind, bend over, and hybridize to adjacent oligos, forming a "bridge."
   - Polymerase extends the bridge, creating a **double-stranded bridge**.
   - Denaturation leaves two copies, each anchored at one end.
   - Repeating 30–35 cycles produces a **clonal cluster** of ~1,000–2,000 identical copies in a tiny spot (~1 µm diameter).
3. **Sequencing-By-Synthesis cycles** (repeated 50–600 times depending on read length):
   - All four RT-dNTPs (each with a different fluorophore and a 3′-blocking group) are added simultaneously.
   - **One** nucleotide is incorporated per DNA strand in each cluster.
   - **Imaging** captures the fluorescence — every cluster's color identifies the base just added.
   - **Cleavage** removes the dye and the 3′-block, restoring a free 3′-OH.
   - Cycle repeats.
4. **Paired-end sequencing**: After the first read, the synthesized strand is washed off and the opposite strand is sequenced from the other end — produces two reads per fragment.

### 2.3 Roche 454 — Pyrosequencing

**Why it works:** When DNA polymerase incorporates a nucleotide, **pyrophosphate (PPi)** is released as a byproduct. PPi is enzymatically converted to light via a **luciferase-driven cascade**. The intensity of light is proportional to the number of nucleotides incorporated in that cycle (homopolymer detection).

**Operational Steps (Logic Bullets):**

1. **Emulsion PCR (emPCR)**: DNA fragments are attached to **microscopic beads** (one fragment per bead). Beads are captured in oil-water micelles (emulsion droplets) containing PCR reagents. After PCR, each bead carries ~10⁶ clonal copies.
2. **Bead deposition**: Beads are loaded into a **PicoTiterPlate (PTP)** — a plate with millions of wells, each holding exactly one bead, plus smaller beads carrying the enzymes (DNA polymerase, ATP-sulfurylase, luciferase, apyrase).
3. **Sequential nucleotide flow**: T, A, C, G are flowed across the plate in a fixed cyclic order (e.g., T → A → C → G → T → A ...).
4. **Light detection**: If the next base to be incorporated matches the flowed nucleotide, PPi is released. The ATP-sulfurylase / luciferase cascade converts PPi into a flash of light. The light intensity (captured by a CCD camera) is **proportional to the number of identical bases incorporated** (the "homopolymer problem").
5. **Apyrase** degrades unincorporated nucleotides before the next flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **The Homopolymer Problem of 454**: If a template contains a run of, say, **6 A's**, the signal strength must be distinguished from runs of 5 or 7. This counting error was the principal weakness of the 454 platform and the main reason it was discontinued.

### 2.4 Applied Biosystems SOLiD — Sequencing-By-Ligation (SBL)

**Why it works:** A **DNA ligase** joins fluorescently labeled **8-mer oligonucleotide probes** to the sequencing primer. The probe's identity (and hence the template bases) is determined by the **fluorescent dye of the first two bases** of the probe.

**Operational Steps (Logic Bullets):**

1. **emPCR on beads**: Same emulsion-PCR strategy as 454 — DNA fragments are clonally amplified on magnetic beads.
2. **Bead deposition** onto a glass slide.
3. **Primer hybridization**: A universal primer anneals to the adapter.
4. **Ligation cycle**:
   - A pool of **16 different 8-mer probes** is added (4 possible first bases × 4 possible second bases).
   - Probes are designed so that positions 1 and 2 carry the fluorophore (4 colors, each representing a **2-base combination**), and positions 3–5 are degenerate randomers, with positions 6–8 as linker/thio-modification.
   - **DNA ligase** joins the matching probe to the primer.
   - The dye identifies the **di-base** at positions 1 and 2.
   - The probe is cleaved (between positions 5 and 6), leaving a 5-base-extended primer.
5. **Reset and shift**: After a series of ligation steps (typically 7–10), the primer is stripped, a primer **offset by one base** is hybridized, and a new ligation round begins. This is repeated for 5 primer offsets.
6. **2-Base Encoding (Color-Space)**: Because each base is interrogated by **two different ligation rounds** (at different primer offsets), every base is read **twice**, providing inherent error correction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Color-Space Notation in SOLiD**: Data is output as a string of fluorescent colors (e.g., 0212310...), not A/T/G/C. A downstream algorithm decodes the color-space into nucleotide sequence. This was both elegant and notoriously difficult for downstream bioinformatics.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Standard Units | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Phred Quality Score** | $Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P_{e})$ | Dimensionless | Base-call confidence metric |
| **Error Probability** | $P_{e} = 10^{-Q/10}$ | Probability (0 to 1) | Used for Q20, Q30, Q40 thresholds |
| **Q20 Accuracy** | $P_{e} = 10^{-20/10} = 10^{-2} = 0.01$ | 99 % accuracy | Acceptable for low-stakes assemblies |
| **Q30 Accuracy** | $P_{e} = 10^{-30/10} = 10^{-3} = 0.001$ | 99.9 % accuracy | **Industry standard cutoff** (Illumina) |
| **Q40 Accuracy** | $P_{e} = 10^{-40/10} = 10^{-4} = 0.0001$ | 99.99 % accuracy | HiFi / clinical-grade sequencing |
| **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = L \times N$ | bases (Mb / Gb / Tb) | Platform capacity benchmarking |
| **Coverage (Depth)** | $C = \dfrac{L \times N}{G}$ | Fold (×) | Statistical sampling of the genome |
| **GC Content** | $\text{GC\%} = \dfrac{G + C}{A + T + G + C} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | Library complexity / bias check |

*Where: $L$ = read length, $N$ = number of reads, $G$ = genome size.*

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Systems

- **Sanger**: Gold standard for **variant validation, clinical confirmation, plasmid verification, and short-amplicon sequencing** (e.g., confirming a CRISPR edit).
- **Illumina**: Dominates **whole-genome sequencing, exome sequencing, RNA-seq, ChIP-seq, ATAC-seq, methylation, and clinical diagnostics**.
- **Roche 454**: Historically dominated **16S rRNA metagenomics** (longer reads resolved closely related species) and **amplicon sequencing** before being overtaken by Illumina and eventually discontinued.
- **SOLiD**: Favored for **resequencing projects** requiring very high accuracy (every base read twice).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Phred Quality Score and Error Probability

The Phred quality score is **empirically defined** as a logarithmic function of the estimated base-calling error probability. The derivation is as follows:

**Step 1 — Empirical Observation:**
A base caller (e.g., the original Phred program) computes a list of peak resolutions, signal-to-noise ratios, and uncalled-coupling parameters. From these, it empirically estimates the probability that the called base is **incorrect**, denoted $P_e$.

**Step 2 — Logarithmic Mapping:**
For intuitive interpretation, the error probability is converted to a logarithmic scale (similar to the decibel scale in electrical engineering). The chosen base is 10 because each integer step in Q corresponds to a 10-fold change in error rate.

$$Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P_e)$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $P_e$:**
Apply the inverse logarithmic transformation:

$$\log_{10}(P_e) = -\dfrac{Q}{10}$$

$$P_e = 10^{-Q/10}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Examples (Exhaustive Calculation):**

*Case 1 — Q20:*

$$P_e = 10^{-20/10} = 10^{-2} = 0.01$$

Accuracy $= 1 - P_e = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99 = 99\%$

*Case 2 — Q30:*

$$P_e = 10^{-30/10} = 10^{-3} = 0.001$$

Accuracy $= 1 - 0.001 = 0.999 = 99.9\%$

*Case 3 — Q40:*

$$P_e = 10^{-40/10} = 10^{-4} = 0.0001$$

Accuracy $= 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999 = 99.99\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Score Thresholds in Practice (KTU Relevance):**
> - **Q ≥ 20**: Generally acceptable for most draft assemblies.
> - **Q ≥ 30**: Standard cutoff for publication-quality Illumina data.
> - **Q ≥ 40**: Required for **clinical diagnostics and FDA-regulated tests**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Coverage (Sequencing Depth)

Coverage is a statistical concept borrowed from signal processing — it tells us how **redundantly** we sampled the genome.

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Coverage $C$ is the average number of times each base in the genome is read.

**Step 2 — Algebraic Setup:**
Let $L$ = average read length (bp), $N$ = total number of reads, and $G$ = haploid genome size (bp).

Each read contributes $L$ bases; therefore, total sequenced bases $= L \times N$.

$$C = \dfrac{\text{Total Sequenced Bases}}{\text{Genome Size}} = \dfrac{L \times N}{G}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example — Human Whole-Genome Sequencing (WGS):**

Suppose an Illumina run produces **1.2 billion paired-end reads** of length **150 bp** (i.e., $N = 1.2 \times 10^{9}$, $L = 150$), and the human haploid genome $G = 3 \times 10^{9}$ bp.

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \dfrac{(1.2 \times 10^{9}) \times 150}{3 \times 10^{9}} \\
  &= \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^{9}} \\
  &= 60 \times
\end{aligned}$$

**Result: 60× coverage** — sufficient for variant calling in a human clinical exome / genome workflow.

**Step 4 — Worked Example — Sanger Low-Throughput Use Case:**

A Sanger capillary run on the 3730xl produces **96 capillaries**, each giving a read of **800 bp**.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput} &= L \times N = 800 \times 96 = 76{,}800 \text{ bp} \approx 77 \text{ kb} \\
C_{E.coli} &= \dfrac{77{,}800}{4.6 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.017 \times
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A single Sanger run covers the *E. coli* genome less than 2 %. This is why the **Sanger method is restricted to targeted sequencing**, not whole-genome work.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Required for KTU 14-Mark Answers)

| Parameter | **Sanger (1st Gen)** | **Illumina (2nd Gen)** | **Roche 454 (2nd Gen)** | **SOLiD (2nd Gen)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Year Introduced** | 1977 | 2006 (Solexa) | 2005 | 2007 |
| **Chemistry Type** | Chain termination | Sequencing-by-synthesis | Pyrosequencing | Sequencing-by-ligation |
| **Amplification** | PCR (single template) | Bridge PCR on flow cell | Emulsion PCR on beads | Emulsion PCR on beads |
| **Read Length** | 500–1000 bp | 50–600 bp (commonly 150–300) | 400–700 bp | 50–75 bp (50+50 paired) |
| **Throughput / Run** | ~0.5–1 Mb | 1.2 Tb (NovaSeq X) | ~0.5–1 Gb | ~300 Gb–1 Tb |
| **Per-Base Accuracy** | Q50 (very high) | Q30–Q40 | Q20 (homopolymer error) | Q35+ (every base twice) |
| **Run Time** | 0.5–3 h | 1–3 days | 10–23 h | 1–7 days |
| **Cost per Mb (USD, ~2024)** | $0.50–1.00 | $0.005–0.01 | $0.10 (historical) | $0.10 (historical) |
| **Key Strength** | Accuracy, long reads, simplicity | Highest throughput, lowest cost | Longest 2nd-gen reads | Highest raw accuracy |
| **Key Weakness** | Very low throughput | Short reads, complex instrument | Homopolymer errors, expensive | Shortest reads, color-space bioinformatics |
| **Current Status** | **Active** (validation/clinical) | **Dominant market leader** | **Discontinued (2016)** | **Discontinued (2017)** |
| **Typical Use Case** | Variant validation, plasmid seq. | WGS, WES, RNA-seq, clinical | 16S amplicon, metagenomics | Resequencing, high-accuracy applications |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** The trade-off between **read length, throughput, accuracy, and cost** is the *defining* comparison axis. Always draw a **2×2 matrix** in the exam showing **read length vs. throughput** to score full marks.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Lander–Waterman Coverage Bound (Conceptual)

The expected number of times a base is sampled follows a **Poisson distribution**. If the mean coverage is $C$, the probability that a base is **not covered** at all is:

$$P(\text{not covered}) = e^{-C}$$

For $C = 30$, $P(\text{not covered}) = e^{-30} \approx 9.36 \times 10^{-14}$ — essentially zero, which is why **30× coverage** is the standard benchmark for human WGS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Note on the Lander–Waterman formula:** $C = L \times N / G$ assumes a **uniform random distribution** of reads across the genome. In practice, **GC bias**, **mappability**, and **PCR duplication** break this assumption, often forcing labs to sequence to **60–100× for clinical-grade** variant calling.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sanger Sequencing — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Template DNA + Primer] --> B[Four Reaction Tubes]
    B --> C1[Tube 1: ddATP]
    B --> C2[Tube 2: ddTTP]
    B --> C3[Tube 3: ddGTP]
    B --> C4[Tube 4: ddCTP]
    C1 --> D[Polymerase Extension: Random Termination]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Population of All Possible Length Fragments]
    E --> F[Capillary Electrophoresis]
    F --> G[Laser Excitation of Fluorescent Labels]
    G --> H[Detector Records Emission Wavelength]
    H --> I[Base-Calling Software Reconstructs Sequence]
    I --> J[Final Electropherogram]
```

### 4.2 Illumina Sequencing-By-Synthesis — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fragmented gDNA with Adapters] --> B[Bind to Flow Cell Oligos]
    B --> C[Bridge Amplification: 30-35 Cycles]
    C --> D[Clonal Cluster of ~1000 Copies]
    D --> E[Add 4 RT-dNTPs and Polymerase]
    E --> F[Single Base Incorporation per Cycle]
    F --> G[Imaging: 4-Channel Fluorescence]
    G --> H[Chemical Cleavage of Dye and 3-prime Block]
    H --> I[Repeat Cycle 50-600 Times]
    I --> J[Base Calls: Image Analysis]
    J --> K[FASTQ Output with Q-scores]
    K --> L{Read 1 Complete}
    L --> M[Regenerate Strand: Read 2 from Opposite End]
    M --> N[Paired-End FASTQ Output]
```

### 4.3 Roche 454 Pyrosequencing — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fragmented DNA Library] --> B[Bind DNA to Microscopic Beads]
    B --> C[Emulsion PCR in Oil-Water Micelles]
    C --> D[Each Bead Carries ~10^6 Clonal Copies]
    D --> E[Load Beads into PicoTiterPlate Wells]
    E --> F[Sequential Flow: T then A then C then G]
    F --> G{Nucleotide Match?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Polymerase Incorporates Base: PPi Released]
    H --> I[ATP-Sulfurylase + Luciferase Cascade Emits Light]
    I --> J[CCD Camera Records Light Intensity]
    G -- No --> K[Apyrase Degrades Unincorporated Nucleotide]
    K --> F
    J --> L[Flow Next Nucleotide]
    L --> F
    L --> M[Reconstruct Sequence from Light Signal Intensity]
```

### 4.4 Applied Biosystems SOLiD — 2-Base Encoding Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fragmented DNA on Beads] --> B[Emulsion PCR Clonal Amplification]
    B --> C[Beads Deposited on Glass Slide]
    C --> D[Universal Primer Hybridizes to Adapter]
    D --> E[Add Pool of 16 Fluorescent 8-mer Probes]
    E --> F[DNA Ligase Joins Matching Probe]
    F --> G[Imaging: 4 Colors Encode 16 Di-nucleotides]
    G --> H[Cleave Probe Between Position 5 and 6]
    H --> I[Repeat Ligation 7-10 Times]
    I --> J[Strip Primer: Reset]
    J --> K[Hybridize Primer Offset by 1 Base]
    K --> L[Run New Ligation Round]
    L --> M{Total 5 Primer Offsets?}
    M -- No --> K
    M -- Yes --> N[Color-Space Output: 0212310...]
    N --> O[Decoder Algorithm Translates to ATGC Sequence]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Platform Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biological Question] --> B{What is the Goal?}
    B -- Validate single variant --> C[Sanger: Highest accuracy, low cost]
    B -- Whole genome or exome --> D[Illumina: High throughput, low cost per base]
    B -- 16S rRNA microbiome amplicon --> E[Roche 454: Long amplicon reads, now replaced by Illumina MiSeq]
    B -- High-accuracy resequencing --> F[SOLiD: Every base read twice, now replaced by Illumina or PacBio HiFi]
    C --> G[Output: Capillary Electrophoresis Trace]
    D --> H[Output: Paired-End FASTQ]
    E --> I[Output: Flowgram with Light Intensities]
    F --> J[Output: Color-Space Reads]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 (3 marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]

> **Q: Define the Phred quality score. What is the minimum Q-score recommended for publication-quality variant calling, and what is the corresponding error probability?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Phred quality score (Q-score)** is a logarithmic measure of the probability that a base call is incorrect, defined as:

$$Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P_e)$$

The **industry-standard minimum Q-score** for publication-quality and clinical variant calling is **Q30**, which corresponds to an error probability of:

$$P_e = 10^{-30/10} = 10^{-3} = 0.001$$

That is, **1 error per 1,000 bases**, equivalent to **99.9 % base-call accuracy**. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for Q30 standard, 1 for error probability calculation.)**

---

**Question 2 (3 marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]

> **Q: What are reversible terminators in Illumina sequencing? Why are they essential for sequencing-by-synthesis?**

**Model Answer:**

**Reversible terminators (RTs)** are modified deoxynucleotide triphosphates (dNTPs) that contain:
1. A **fluorescent dye** attached to the base (distinct fluorophore for A, T, G, C).
2. A **chemically cleavable 3′-OH blocking group** (e.g., a small azidomethyl group).

**Why they are essential:**
- They ensure that during each cycle, **only ONE base is incorporated** by the DNA polymerase per template strand — preventing asynchronous extension that would scramble the read.
- After imaging, the **3′-block and the dye are chemically cleaved**, restoring a free 3′-OH for the next cycle.
- This allows the cyclic, controlled, **base-by-base readout** that defines sequencing-by-synthesis. **(3 marks: 1 for structural description, 1 for the role of the 3′-block, 1 for the cyclical cleaving mechanism.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 1 Internal Choice**: Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **principle, workflow, and chemistry of Sanger sequencing**. Add a note on the role of ddNTPs and how modern dye-terminator sequencing replaced the four-tube method.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Sanger sequencing with Illumina sequencing** across read length, throughput, cost per base, accuracy, and typical applications. Use a suitable table or matrix.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
Sanger sequencing, developed by **Frederick Sanger in 1977**, is the **chain-termination method**. It relies on the incorporation of **2′,3′-dideoxynucleoside triphosphates (ddNTPs)** by DNA polymerase. Unlike normal dNTPs, ddNTPs **lack a free 3′-hydroxyl group**, so once incorporated, no further phosphodiester bond can form and the elongating strand is **terminated permanently**.

**Workflow (3 marks):**
1. A single-stranded DNA template is hybridized with a sequencing primer.
2. (Classical) Four separate reaction tubes are prepared — each contains DNA polymerase, all four dNTPs, and **one labeled ddNTP** (ddATP, ddTTP, ddGTP, or ddCTP).
3. During extension, random incorporation of ddNTP terminates some chains. The result is a population of strands spanning **every possible length**, each ending in a specific base.
4. The products are separated by **capillary electrophoresis** (modern) or polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (original Sanger).
5. A **laser excites the fluorescent labels**, and a detector records the emission wavelength as fragments migrate past.
6. The **electropherogram** is converted to a nucleotide sequence by base-calling software.

**Chemistry of ddNTPs (1 mark):**
The ddNTP competes with its normal dNTP counterpart. Because the ratio of ddNTP:dNTP is carefully tuned (~1 %), a ladder of terminations is produced — long enough to be informative and frequent enough to be statistically sampled.

**Modern Dye-Terminator Upgrade (1 mark):**
In the 1990s, **single-tube dye-terminator sequencing** was introduced: all four ddNTPs are in one reaction, each labeled with a **distinct fluorescent dye**. This eliminated the need to run four lanes in parallel, simplified automation, and improved read length (up to 1000 bp on the 3730xl). Applied Biosystems / Thermo Fisher still sells Sanger capillary instruments used in clinical confirmatory testing.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | **Sanger (1st Gen)** | **Illumina (2nd Gen)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Read Length** | 500–1000 bp | 50–600 bp (typically 150–300) |
| **Throughput per Run** | ~0.5–1 Mb (96 capillaries) | Up to 1.2 Tb (NovaSeq X) |
| **Per-Base Accuracy** | Q50 (99.999 %) | Q30–Q40 (99.9–99.99 %) |
| **Cost per Mb (USD)** | $0.50–1.00 | $0.005–0.01 |
| **Sample Multiplexing** | Low (1–96 per run) | Very high (hundreds of samples via index barcodes) |
| **Typical Application** | Single-variant validation, plasmid confirmation, clinical confirmation | Whole-genome, exome, RNA-seq, ChIP-seq, methylation, clinical NGS |
| **Instrument Cost** | ~$50,000–$100,000 | $100,000 (MiSeq) – $1,000,000+ (NovaSeq X) |
| **Run Time** | 0.5–3 h | 1–3 days |

**Analytical Conclusion (2 marks):**
Sanger is unbeatable for **accuracy and read length on a single template** but cannot scale economically. Illumina sacrifices read length for **massive parallelism and dramatic cost reduction**, making it the dominant platform for population-scale genomics. The two technologies are **complementary, not competitors** — modern clinical pipelines use Illumina for discovery and Sanger for confirmation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Illumina sequencing-by-synthesis (SBS) workflow** in detail. Explain bridge amplification, cluster generation, the role of reversible terminators, and paired-end sequencing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principles of Roche 454 pyrosequencing and Applied Biosystems SOLiD sequencing-by-ligation**. Discuss the homopolymer problem in 454 and the 2-base encoding advantage of SOLiD.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Library Preparation (1 mark):**
Genomic DNA is fragmented (enzymatically or by sonication) to a target size (typically 200–600 bp). **Adapters** are ligated to both fragment ends. These adapters contain:
- Sequences complementary to the **flow-cell oligos** (for surface binding).
- **Index/barcode sequences** (for sample multiplexing).
- Primer-binding sites for sequencing.

**Bridge Amplification and Cluster Generation (2.5 marks):**
The flow cell is a glass slide with **8 channels**, each coated with a lawn of two types of oligonucleotides complementary to the two adapter ends. Steps:
1. The library is injected; one adapter of each fragment anneals to a complementary oligo.
2. DNA polymerase extends the oligo, creating a complementary strand covalently attached to the flow cell.
3. The original template is denatured and washed away.
4. The newly synthesized strand bends over ("bridges") and anneals to a second oligo on the flow-cell surface.
5. Polymerase extends the bridge, forming a **double-stranded bridge**.
6. Denaturation yields two copies, each anchored to the surface.
7. Repeating this 30–35 times produces a **clonal cluster** of ~1,000–2,000 identical copies in a spot ~1 µm in diameter.
8. One strand is cleaved to leave only the **forward-strand clusters** for sequencing (and the other is reserved for paired-end).

**Sequencing-By-Synthesis with Reversible Terminators (2.5 marks):**
Each sequencing cycle:
1. All four RT-dNTPs (each with a distinct fluorophore and a 3′-blocking group) are flowed across the flow cell.
2. Polymerase incorporates **one** RT-dNTP per template strand — the 3′-block prevents further extension.
3. **Imaging**: A camera captures fluorescence in 4 channels; each cluster's color identifies the base just added.
4. **Cleavage**: The dye and the 3′-block are chemically removed, restoring a free 3′-OH.
5. The cycle repeats for 50–600 cycles, depending on the desired read length.

**Paired-End Sequencing (1 mark):**
After the forward read, the template is washed off. The cluster's reverse strand (which was retained on the flow cell) is used to perform a **second read from the opposite end**. This produces two reads per fragment with a known approximate insert size, which:
- Improves variant calling accuracy.
- Enables detection of **structural variants, indels, and splice junctions** in RNA-seq.
- Doubles effective coverage per fragment.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Roche 454 Pyrosequencing (3.5 marks):**

*Principle:* Each nucleotide incorporation by DNA polymerase releases **pyrophosphate (PPi)**. A coupled enzymatic cascade converts PPi into ATP, which is then used by **firefly luciferase** to oxidize luciferin and emit light. The **light intensity is proportional to the number of bases incorporated** in a homopolymer run.

*Workflow:*
1. **Emulsion PCR (emPCR):** DNA fragments are attached to ~28-µm beads (one fragment per bead). The beads are emulsified in an oil-water mixture containing PCR reagents. After thermal cycling, each bead carries ~10⁶ clonal copies.
2. **Bead deposition:** Beads are deposited into a **PicoTiterPlate (PTP)** — a fused-fiber plate with millions of ~44-µm wells, each sized to hold one bead. Smaller beads carrying the four enzymes (polymerase, ATP-sulfurylase, luciferase, apyrase) are co-loaded.
3. **Sequential flow:** The four nucleotides are flowed one at a time in a fixed order. If the flowed nucleotide matches the next base on the template, the cascade emits light; if not, **apyrase degrades** the unincorporated nucleotide before the next flow.
4. A **CCD camera** captures the light intensity at every well after every flow; the data form a **flowgram**, from which the base sequence is reconstructed.

*Homopolymer problem:* If the template contains a run of 6 A's, the signal must be distinguished from runs of 5 or 7 A's. Counting errors are the main source of indels in 454 data. This weakness is **inherent to the chemistry** because the signal is a scalar quantity (light intensity) rather than a discrete event.

**SOLiD Sequencing-By-Ligation (3.5 marks):**

*Principle:* A **DNA ligase** joins fluorescently labeled **8-mer oligonucleotide probes** to a sequencing primer. The fluorescent color encodes the **first two bases** of the probe — there are **16 possible di-nucleotides** but only **4 fluorophores**, so each color represents 4 di-nucleotides (e.g., a "blue" probe might be AA, CC, GG, or TT — distinguished by other ligation rounds).

*Workflow:*
1. **emPCR** on magnetic beads (same concept as 454) clonal-amplifies fragments.
2. Beads are deposited on a glass slide.
3. A **universal primer** (P1) anneals to the adapter; the **first round of ligation** uses probes interrogating positions 1–2 of the insert.
4. After ligation, the dye is imaged, then the probe is **cleaved between positions 5 and 6** (a thiol linker), leaving a 5-base-extended primer. This is repeated 7–10 times.
5. The primer is stripped, a **new primer offset by one base** (P2) is hybridized, and another round of ligation-imaging-cleavage is performed.
6. The process is repeated for **5 different primer offsets** (P1, P2, P3, P4, P5) — each offset shifts the read frame by one base, so every base is read **twice** in two different ligation rounds.
7. The output is a string of **colors (color-space)**, e.g., `0212310...`. A downstream algorithm uses the **2-base encoding rules** to convert color-space into nucleotide sequence (A, T, G, C).

*2-Base encoding advantage:* Because every base is interrogated twice (at two different primer offsets), **single-base errors can be bioinformatically distinguished from true variants**, providing the highest raw accuracy of any 2nd-gen platform.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Module 1 Long Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Sanger sequencing" with "next-generation sequencing."** Sanger is 1st generation, not NGS. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this basic error.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of the 3′-OH block in reversible terminators.** Many students describe the dye but forget the 3′-blocking group — both are required for full marks on Illumina.
> 3. **Stating "454 reads single molecules" — false.** All 2nd-gen platforms (454, SOLiD, Illumina) require **clonal amplification**; only 3rd-gen platforms (PacBio, Nanopore) are single-molecule.
> 4. **Confusing color-space with base-space in SOLiD.** SOLiD does **not** directly output A/T/G/C; it outputs a color string. Markers want this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the homopolymer problem in 454.** This is the most-asked concept question on 454 in KTU papers. Not mentioning it typically costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **No comparison table in a "compare" question.** A 7-mark compare question **must** include a structured table or matrix — narrative-only answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Wrong formula for Phred score.** Write it as $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$ — not $\log_{10}$ base e, not $-10 \log_e$, not $10 \log_{10}$.
> 8. **Forgetting that all three NGS platforms (Illumina, 454, SOLiD) have been largely displaced for routine use.** Illumina dominates; 454 and SOLiD are mostly **discontinued** — the examiner may ask "Why was 454 discontinued?" and expect a homopolymer-error answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Sanger sequencing (1st gen)** uses **chain termination by ddNTPs**; **500–1000 bp** reads; **Q50** accuracy; **<1 Mb/run** throughput; used today for **variant validation and clinical confirmation**.
- [ ] **Illumina (2nd gen)** uses **bridge PCR cluster amplification** on a flow cell and **reversible terminator SBS**; **Q30+** accuracy; **up to 1.2 Tb/run** on NovaSeq X; **current market leader** in NGS.
- [ ] **Roche 454 (2nd gen)** uses **emulsion PCR on beads** and **pyrosequencing (luciferase light detection)**; **400–700 bp** reads (longest in 2nd gen); **discontinued in 2016** due to the **homopolymer indels** problem.
- [ ] **Applied Biosystems SOLiD (2nd gen)** uses **emulsion PCR on beads** and **sequencing-by-ligation** with **2-base color encoding**; **every base read twice** → highest raw accuracy; **discontinued in 2017**; output is **color-space, not base-space**.
- [ ] All 2nd-gen platforms perform **clonal amplification** of DNA fragments **before** sequencing; only 3rd-gen platforms (SMRT, Nanopore) sequence single molecules.
- [ ] **Phred quality score formula:** $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$; **Q30 = 99.9 % accuracy** is the publication/clinical benchmark.
- [ ] **Coverage formula:** $C = (L \times N) / G$; **30× coverage** is the standard for human WGS; clinical workflows often use **60–100×**.
- [ ] **Trade-off principle:** Sequencing platforms balance **read length ↔ throughput ↔ accuracy ↔ cost** — no single platform wins on all four axes.
- [ ] **454 → SOLiD → Illumina** is the **historical dominance timeline** of 2nd-gen sequencing (2005 → 2007 → 2010–present).
- [ ] **Paired-end Illumina sequencing** reads both ends of a fragment → improves variant calling, indel detection, and splice-junction inference.
- [ ] **PicoTiterPlate (PTP)** and **emulsion PCR (emPCR)** are signature 454 technologies; **Bridge amplification** and **reversible terminators** are signature Illumina technologies; **2-base color-space probes** are signature SOLiD technology.
- [ ] For a **14-mark compare question**, always include a **structured table** and a **concluding analytical paragraph** to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Helicos Biosciences, Oxford Nanopore, and Pacific BioSciences - Principles, Advantages, and Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT755 - NEXT GENERATION SEQUENCING TECHNOLOGIES: DATA ANALYSIS AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Helicos Biosciences, Oxford Nanopore, and Pacific BioSciences - Principles, Advantages, and Challenges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Overview of Third-Generation Sequencing (TGS) Technologies

Third-Generation Sequencing (TGS) represents a paradigm shift in genomics. Unlike Second-Generation Sequencing (SGS) platforms (Illumina, Ion Torrent) which rely on **PCR amplification** and **short-read lengths** (typically 100–300 bp), TGS platforms sequence **single DNA molecules in real time** without amplification, generating **long reads** that can span repetitive regions and structural variants.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Third-Generation Sequencing refers to single-molecule, real-time sequencing technologies that bypass library amplification and produce read lengths typically in the range of **10 kb to >2 Mb**, enabling direct detection of epigenetic modifications and native nucleic acid analysis.

The three pioneering TGS platforms covered in this module are:

| Platform | Manufacturer | Key Innovation |
|----------|--------------|----------------|
| **HeliScope (tSMS)** | Helicos Biosciences (Defunct, 2012) | First true single-molecule sequencer |
| **MinION/GridION/PromethION** | Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT) | Electrical signal-based nanopore detection |
| **PacBio Sequel II/Sequel IIe/Revio** | Pacific BioSciences (PacBio) | Zero-Mode Waveguide + SMRT sequencing |

---

### 1.2 Helicos BioSciences — HeliScope (True Single-Molecule Sequencing)

**Formal Definition:**
True Single-Molecule Sequencing (tSMS) developed by Helicos BioSciences was the **first commercial platform to sequence individual DNA molecules without amplification**. It employed a cyclical approach of single-nucleotide addition using fluorescently labeled virtual terminators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Helicos BioSciences filed for bankruptcy in **November 2012**, making the HeliScope historically significant but commercially obsolete. The technology paved the way for all subsequent single-molecule sequencing efforts.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Microscopic Typewriter:**
Imagine a microscopic surface covered with millions of tiny **"typing stations"**, each holding a single strand of DNA. A DNA polymerase acts like a typist that adds one fluorescently labeled "letter" (nucleotide) at a time. After every keystroke, a camera takes a snapshot of the entire page, identifies which letter was added at each station, and then the fluorescent tag is chemically cleaved so the typist can add the next letter. Unlike a regular typewriter that requires a carbon copy (PCR amplification), this machine reads the **original document directly** — one molecule at a time.

---

### 1.3 Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT) — Nanopore Sequencing

**Formal Definition:**
Nanopore sequencing is a **single-molecule, real-time electrical detection method** that measures disruptions in ionic current as nucleic acid molecules pass through a biological or solid-state nanoscale pore (typically **~1–2 nm** diameter) embedded in a resistive membrane.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Thread Through a Needle:**
Picture a microscopic needle with a hole just wide enough to pass a single strand of DNA — like threading a thread through the eye of a needle, but in reverse. As the DNA strand squeezes through this nanopore, it partially **blocks the flow of electrically charged ions** (like a plug in a pipe). Each of the four nucleotide bases (A, T, G, C) blocks the current by a slightly different amount, creating a unique electrical "fingerprint." A sensitive ammeter records these fluctuations, and a computer decodes the squiggle patterns back into the DNA sequence — much like reading Morse code from electrical pulses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant:** The applied voltage across the nanopore membrane is typically **~180 mV to +300 mV**, and the resulting current through an open pore is approximately **~100–300 pA** depending on pore chemistry.

---

### 1.4 Pacific BioSciences (PacBio) — SMRT Sequencing

**Formal Definition:**
Single Molecule Real-Time (SMRT) sequencing by Pacific BioSciences uses **Zero-Mode Waveguides (ZMWs)** — nanoscale wells of ~70 nm diameter — to confine fluorescent excitation to a volume of **~20 zeptoliters (10⁻²¹ L)**, allowing real-time observation of single DNA polymerase molecules incorporating fluorescently labeled nucleotides.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Movie Theater with Tiny Seats:**
Think of a movie theater where each seat is so small (a ZMW) that only one customer (a DNA polymerase) can sit in it, and the projector light is so tightly focused that it only illuminates that single seat. A special 3D movie plays out: as the polymerase adds each new nucleotide, a flash of colored light bursts on the screen. Cameras record this movie continuously, frame by frame, capturing every single enzymatic step in real time. Because the entire light show is happening in ultra-confined spaces, **billions of these movies can run in parallel on a single chip**.

> [!NOTE]
> PacBio's high-fidelity (HiFi) reads achieve **>99.9% accuracy (Q30)** by using **Circular Consensus Sequencing (CCS)**, where a single SMRTbell molecule is read multiple times to generate a consensus.

---

### 1.5 Why These Technologies Matter — KTU Engineering Perspective

From a KTU bioinformatics/biotechnology engineering perspective, these platforms are critical because they:

- Solve the **"long-read problem"** of short-read Illumina sequencing (reads < 300 bp).
- Enable **de novo assembly** of complex genomes (e.g., the human T2T genome completed in 2022 used PacBio + ONT).
- Allow direct detection of **base modifications** (methylation) without bisulfite conversion.
- Generate data with fundamentally different error profiles and computational challenges.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Helicos HeliScope — Operational Theory

#### 2.1.1 Surface Chemistry & Library Prep

1. DNA is sheared into fragments of **~100–200 bp** average length.
2. A **poly-A tail** is added to the 3' end of each fragment using terminal transferase.
3. The poly-A-tailed fragments hybridize to **dT50 oligomers** covalently attached to a glass flow cell surface — **one fragment per oligo spot**.
4. The flow cell contains approximately **~50 million oligo-dT capture sites** per channel.

#### 2.1.2 Sequencing-by-Synthesis Cycle

1. DNA polymerase and one of four **fluorescently labeled virtual terminators** (Cy5, Cy3, Alexa-555, Alexa-546) are flowed in.
2. The polymerase incorporates a single base complementary to the template.
3. **Total Internal Reflection Fluorescence (TIRF) microscopy** excites and images the surface.
4. The fluorophore + terminator group is cleaved using **Tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine (TCEP)**.
5. Cycle repeats for the next base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each cycle produces **1 base per fragment**. To sequence 30 bases, **30 cycles** are required. The HeliScope could run **~120 cycles per day** for a typical run of 8 days.

#### 2.1.3 Why "True" Single-Molecule?

- **No PCR amplification** → no amplification bias, no GC-bias artifacts.
- **No clonal population** required → single DNA molecule is sequenced directly.
- **Coverage is determined by** the number of captured fragments, not by PCR duplicates.

---

### 2.2 Oxford Nanopore — Operational Theory

#### 2.2.1 The Physics of Nanopore Detection

A nanopore is immersed in an electrolyte solution (typically **KCl, 250–500 mM**). When a voltage is applied across the membrane:

$$I = \frac{V}{R_{pore}}$$

where:
- $I$ = ionic current (amperes)
- $V$ = applied transmembrane potential (volts)
- $R_{pore}$ = pore resistance (ohms)

When a single DNA strand enters the pore, it displaces a fraction of the electrolyte ions. The residual current is:

$$I_{residual} = I_{open} \cdot (1 - \alpha_{kmer})$$

where $\alpha_{kmer}$ is a **kmer-specific current scaling factor** for the k nucleotides currently occupying the pore sensing region (typically **k = 5 to 6 bases** for standard ONT basecallers).

#### 2.2.2 Pore Chemistries

| Pore Type | Used In | Enzyme | Application |
|-----------|---------|--------|-------------|
| **R6 / R7** | Older MinION | None (strand-based) | DNA, cDNA |
| **R9.4 / R10.4** | MinION/GridION/PromethION | None | DNA sequencing |
| **RNA pore** | MinION | RNase | Direct RNA sequencing |

#### 2.2.3 Basecalling Models

- **Older (ONT legacy):** Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) → event detection.
- **Modern (since 2020):** Deep neural networks (e.g., Guppy, Dorado, Bonito).
- **Output:** FASTQ files with basecalls + quality scores (Q-scores).
- **Raw data:** FAST5 or POD5 files containing the squiggle signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern ONT basecallers (Dorado) can produce **Q20+ accuracy** (99% accuracy) and **Q30+** (99.9% accuracy) on duplex/consensus protocols.

#### 2.2.4 Library Prep — Ligation-Based (LSK)

1. DNA end-repair + dA-tailing.
2. **Motor protein** (e.g., E. coli phage WGA Cas9 coupling) ligated to adapter.
3. Tether attachment at the other end prevents pore escape.
4. **Read length** is set primarily by fragment size + motor protein processivity.

#### 2.2.5 Library Prep — Rapid (Transposase-Based)

Uses a **Tnp transposase** to fragment DNA and attach adapters in a single 5-minute reaction — ideal for **field-deployable** MinION use.

---

### 2.3 Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) — Operational Theory

#### 2.3.1 Zero-Mode Waveguide (ZMW) Physics

A ZMW is an aluminum-clad hole of **~70 nm diameter** etched into a fused silica substrate. Light at 600–800 nm cannot propagate through a structure smaller than its wavelength, so the excitation light decays **evanescently** into the well:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z/\lambda_{evanescent}}$$

This confines fluorescence excitation to a **~20 zL** observation volume, allowing detection of single polymerase molecules immobilized at the bottom of each ZMW.

> [!NOTE]
> A SMRT Cell contains **~8 million ZMWs** (Sequel II) or **~25–50 million** (Revio).

#### 2.3.2 Phospholink Chemistry

- Nucleotides are labeled on the **phosphate group** (not the base).
- Upon incorporation, **pyrophosphate + dye** is cleaved and diffuses out of the ZMW.
- This naturally **terminates the signal** (no chemical cleavage step needed) and allows **continuous, long reads**.

#### 2.3.3 SMRTbell Template

PacBio uses a **circular, topologically closed template** called a SMRTbell:

- Insert DNA is ligated between two **SMRTbell adapters** (hairpin loops).
- The polymerase goes around continuously: insert → adapter → insert → adapter → ...
- This produces a **Continuous Long Read (CLR)**.

#### 2.3.4 Circular Consensus Sequencing (CCS) — HiFi

For HiFi reads, the polymerase is allowed to make **multiple full passes** around the SMRTbell:

$$N_{subreads} = \frac{L_{polymerase\ read}}{L_{insert} + L_{adapters}}$$

A consensus is then built:

$$Q_{consensus} \approx -\frac{10 \cdot \log_{10}(E_{per\ pass}) \cdot \sqrt{N_{subreads}}}{N_{subreads}}$$

- Each subread is ~**Q8–Q12** (raw single-pass accuracy).
- HiFi consensus of **10–15 passes** → **Q30+ accuracy** (>99.9%).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Value / Unit | Platform |
|---------------------|--------|--------------|----------|
| Open pore current | $I_{open}$ | **~100–300 pA** | ONT |
| Applied voltage | $V$ | **+180 to +300 mV** | ONT |
| Pore sensing region | $k$ | **5–6 bases** (kmer) | ONT |
| ZMW diameter | $d$ | **~70 nm** | PacBio |
| ZMW observation volume | $V_{obs}$ | **~20 zL (10⁻²¹ L)** | PacBio |
| Evanescent decay length | $\lambda_{evanescent}$ | **~20–30 nm** | PacBio |
| HeliScope cycles/day | $n_{cyc}$ | **~120** | Helicos |
| HeliScope flow cell channels | $n_{ch}$ | **25** | Helicos |
| PacBio Sequel II ZMWs/cell | $N_{ZMW}$ | **~8 million** | PacBio |
| PacBio Revio SMRT Cells | $N_{cells}$ | **4 per run** | PacBio |
| HiFi accuracy (consensus) | $Q$ | **Q30+ (>99.9%)** | PacBio |
| ONT read length (ultra-long) | $L_{read}$ | **>2 Mb possible** | ONT |
| PacBio CLR read length | $L_{read}$ | **10–30 kb typical** | PacBio |
| HeliScope read length | $L_{read}$ | **25–55 bp** | Helicos |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse HeliScope's **25–55 bp short reads** with PacBio's **10–30 kb long reads** or ONT's **>2 Mb ultra-long reads**. The "single-molecule" descriptor does NOT mean "long reads" — Helicos reads were actually shorter than Illumina v1!

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Bioinformatics

| Application | Helicos (Historical) | ONT (Modern) | PacBio (Modern) |
|-------------|----------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Whole genome de novo assembly | Limited | Excellent | Excellent |
| Structural variant (SV) calling | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Full-length isoform sequencing (Iso-Seq) | No | Yes (direct RNA) | Yes (Iso-Seq) |
| Methylation detection | No | Yes (native) | Yes (kinetics) |
| Pathogen surveillance (real-time) | No | **Yes** (ARTIC, e.g., COVID-19 UK surveillance) | Limited |
| Field-deployable sequencing | No | **Yes** (MinION, USB-sized) | No |
| Closing reference gaps / T2T genomes | No | Yes | Yes |
| Ancient DNA (aDNA) recovery | No | Yes (ultra-long) | Yes (HiFi) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Nanopore Current Modulation Model

The classic model of nanopore current modulation treats the pore as having a sensing region of length $\ell$ (in nm) and radius $r_{pore}$. A DNA strand of radius $r_{DNA}$ ($\approx$ 1.1 nm) partially occupies this region, displacing ions.

**Step 1: Conductance of Open Pore**

The conductance $G_{open}$ of an open cylindrical pore is given by:

$$G_{open} = \sigma_{KCl} \cdot \pi \cdot \frac{r_{pore}^{2}}{L_{pore}}$$

where $\sigma_{KCl}$ is the bulk electrolyte conductivity (S/m), $r_{pore}$ is the pore radius, and $L_{pore}$ is the effective pore length.

**Step 2: Conductance of Occupied Pore**

When a DNA strand of radius $r_{DNA}$ occupies the sensing region of length $\ell$, the effective cross-section available for ionic flow is reduced:

$$G_{occupied} = \sigma_{KCl} \cdot \pi \cdot \frac{(r_{pore}^{2} - r_{DNA}^{2})}{\ell} + \sigma_{KCl} \cdot \pi \cdot \frac{r_{pore}^{2}}{(L_{pore} - \ell)}$$

**Step 3: Residual Current Ratio**

Define the residual current as:

$$I_{residual} = \frac{G_{occupied}}{G_{open}} \cdot I_{open}$$

**Step 4: Base Discrimination via Residual Current**

Each k-mer (5–6 bases) produces a characteristic residual current $I_{res}$:

$$I_{res, kmer} = I_{open} \cdot \frac{\ell \cdot (r_{pore}^{2} - r_{DNA}^{2}) + r_{pore}^{2} \cdot (L_{pore} - \ell)}{L_{pore} \cdot r_{pore}^{2}}$$

**Step 5: Numerical Illustration (Cis-104 pore, typical values)**

Let $\sigma_{KCl} = 4.5$ S/m, $r_{pore} = 0.7$ nm, $r_{DNA} = 1.1$ nm (note DNA radius is larger than pore radius → DNA must thread linearly), $L_{pore} = 5$ nm, $\ell = 0.5$ nm:

Numerator: $0.5 \cdot (0.49 - 1.21) + 0.49 \cdot 4.5 = 0.5 \cdot (-0.72) + 2.205 = -0.36 + 2.205 = 1.845$

Denominator: $5 \cdot 0.49 = 2.45$

$$\frac{I_{res}}{I_{open}} \approx \frac{1.845}{2.45} \approx 0.753$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the residual current is dominated by **access resistance** and **electro-osmotic effects**, making the model above a simplified first-order approximation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — PacBio HiFi Consensus Accuracy

**Step 1: Single-Pass Accuracy**

The raw accuracy of a PacBio CLR subread is approximately:

$$A_{single} = 1 - e_{per\ base} \approx 0.85 \text{ to } 0.90 \text{ (i.e., Q8 to Q10)}$$

So per-base error rate $\epsilon \approx 0.10$ to $0.15$.

**Step 2: Multi-Pass Consensus**

If the polymerase makes $N$ independent passes around the SMRTbell, each base is sequenced $N$ times. Assume errors are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) with per-base error rate $\epsilon$.

Probability of **at least one error** across $N$ passes:

$$P(\text{at least 1 error}) = 1 - (1-\epsilon)^{N}$$

The **expected number of correct calls** if we take the majority vote is approximately:

$$P(\text{majority correct}) = \sum_{k=\lceil N/2 \rceil}^{N} \binom{N}{k} (1-\epsilon)^{k} \epsilon^{N-k}$$

**Step 3: Q-Score Conversion**

$$Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(1 - P(\text{majority correct}))$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example**

For $N = 10$ passes and $\epsilon = 0.10$:

$$P(\text{correct, single}) = 0.90$$

$$P(\text{at least 1 error, 10 passes}) = 1 - 0.90^{10} = 1 - 0.3487 = 0.6513$$

$$P(\text{majority of 10 correct}) = \sum_{k=5}^{10} \binom{10}{k} (0.90)^{k} (0.10)^{10-k}$$

Compute term-by-term:
- $k=5$: $\binom{10}{5} \cdot 0.9^{5} \cdot 0.1^{5} = 252 \cdot 0.59049 \cdot 0.00001 \approx 0.00149$
- $k=6$: $210 \cdot 0.531441 \cdot 0.000001 \approx 0.0112$
- $k=7$: $120 \cdot 0.4782969 \cdot 10^{-7} \approx 0.00574$
- $k=8$: $45 \cdot 0.43046721 \cdot 10^{-8} \approx 0.00194$
- $k=9$: $10 \cdot 0.387420489 \cdot 10^{-9} \approx 0.0000039$
- $k=10$: $1 \cdot 0.3486784401 \cdot 10^{-10} \approx 3.49 \times 10^{-11}$

**Total:** $P(\text{majority correct}) \approx 0.0204$

Wait — this is the wrong calculation! For majority vote, we need **k > 5 errors** meaning **k > 5 mistakes**, i.e., we need **k > 5 errors**, so we need k ≤ 5 errors for majority to be correct. Let me redo:

For majority correct: $k \leq 4$ errors out of $N=10$:

$$P(\text{majority correct}) = \sum_{k=0}^{4} \binom{10}{k} (0.9)^{10-k} (0.1)^{k}$$

- $k=0$: $1 \cdot 0.9^{10} \cdot 1 = 0.3487$
- $k=1$: $10 \cdot 0.9^{9} \cdot 0.1 = 10 \cdot 0.3874 \cdot 0.1 = 0.3874$
- $k=2$: $45 \cdot 0.9^{8} \cdot 0.01 = 45 \cdot 0.4305 \cdot 0.01 = 0.1937$
- $k=3$: $120 \cdot 0.9^{7} \cdot 0.001 = 120 \cdot 0.4783 \cdot 0.001 = 0.0574$
- $k=4$: $210 \cdot 0.9^{6} \cdot 0.0001 = 210 \cdot 0.5314 \cdot 0.0001 = 0.01116$

**Total:** $P(\text{majority correct}) \approx 0.9984$

Therefore:
$$Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(1 - 0.9984) = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(0.0016) = -10 \cdot (-2.796) \approx 27.96$$

This explains why PacBio HiFi requires **≥10 passes** to reach Q30. With more passes (e.g., 20+), accuracy climbs to Q40+.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Three Platforms

| Feature | Helicos (tSMS) | PacBio (SMRT) | Oxford Nanopore |
|---------|----------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Year commercialized** | 2008 | 2011 | 2014 |
| **Read length** | 25–55 bp | 10–30 kb (CLR); HiFi = 10–25 kb | 1 kb–2 Mb (ultra-long) |
| **Throughput per run** | ~21–35 Gb | ~500 Gb (Revio) | ~50–290 Gb (PromethION) |
| **Per-base accuracy** | ~97% (Q17) | HiFi Q30+; CLR Q10 | Q20–Q30 (consensus) |
| **Single molecule?** | Yes (true) | Yes | Yes |
| **PCR amplification?** | No | No | No |
| **Error type** | Indels & substitutions | Indels (CLR); low (HiFi) | Indels + homopolymer errors |
| **Detection method** | Fluorescence (TIRF) | Fluorescence (ZMW) | Electrical (current) |
| **Output format** | Helicos QSEQ, FASTQ | BAM/FASTQ, CCS reads | FAST5/POD5, FASTQ |
| **Detects base mods?** | No | Yes (kinetics) | Yes (signal-based) |
| **Real-time analysis?** | No | No (post-run) | **Yes** (live basecalling) |
| **Device size** | Benchtop (large) | Benchtop (large) | **MinION: 90g, USB** |
| **Status (2024)** | **Defunct** | Active | Active |
| **Cost per Gb (approx.)** | N/A (defunct) | ~$7–$15 | ~$1–$10 |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP PEBBT755]`

**Q: What is True Single-Molecule Sequencing (tSMS)? Name the platform that pioneered this technology.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
True Single-Molecule Sequencing (tSMS) is a sequencing method in which **individual DNA molecules are sequenced directly without any prior amplification (e.g., PCR) or clonal generation**. Each DNA fragment is captured on a solid surface (typically via poly-A / oligo-dT hybridization) and sequenced by cyclical incorporation of single fluorescently labeled virtual terminators detected by TIRF microscopy. The **HeliScope** sequencer developed by **Helicos BioSciences** (commercialized 2008) pioneered this technology. The platform generated 25–55 bp reads with ~Q17 accuracy (~97%), and though commercially defunct after Helicos' 2012 bankruptcy, it laid the foundation for all subsequent single-molecule platforms.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP PEBBT755]`

**Q: Briefly explain the principle of Oxford Nanopore sequencing. Mention any one advantage of this technology.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Oxford Nanopore sequencing measures changes in **ionic current** as a single DNA strand passes through a **biological nanopore** (typically ~1–2 nm diameter) embedded in a polymer membrane. Each k-mer of 5–6 nucleotides produces a characteristic current signature, which a neural network basecaller (e.g., Dorado) translates into basecalls.

**Advantage (any one):**
- Generates **ultra-long reads (>2 Mb)** enabling resolution of complex repetitive regions and structural variants.
- **Portable** (MinION is USB-sized, ~90 g), enabling field and real-time sequencing (e.g., the COVID-19 ARTIC network).
- **Direct detection of base modifications** (e.g., 5mC, 6mA) without chemical conversion.
- **No PCR amplification** required, eliminating amplification bias.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP PEBBT755]`

**Q: Describe the principles, working mechanism, advantages, and challenges of Pacific BioSciences Single Molecule Real-Time (SMRT) sequencing technology.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle and Working Mechanism (7 marks)**

SMRT sequencing is based on the **single-molecule, real-time** observation of DNA polymerase activity using **Zero-Mode Waveguides (ZMWs)**.

**Step-by-step mechanism:**

1. **Library Preparation:** High-molecular-weight DNA is fragmented to a target size (typically 10–25 kb). End repair and SMRTbell ligation produce **topologically circular templates** with hairpin adapters on both ends.

2. **ZMW Loading:** A SMRT Cell contains millions of ZMWs — aluminum-clad nanoholes of ~70 nm diameter etched into fused silica. Each ZMW is loaded with a single DNA polymerase immobilized at the bottom via a biotin-streptavidin linkage, and one SMRTbell template per ZMW.

3. **Evanescent Wave Excitation:** Light of ~600 nm wavelength cannot propagate through a 70 nm aperture. Instead, it decays **evanescently** with a characteristic length of **~20–30 nm**, confining fluorescence excitation to a tiny ~**20 zeptoliter (10⁻²¹ L)** observation volume. This allows single-molecule detection without background fluorescence.

4. **Phospholink Chemistry:** Each of the four dNTPs is labeled with a distinct fluorophore on the **terminal phosphate (γ or β)**. Upon incorporation by the polymerase, pyrophosphate + dye is released and diffuses out of the ZMW — naturally terminating the fluorescent pulse.

5. **Optical Detection:** A high-sensitivity camera records the **pulse color (base identity), pulse duration (interpulse duration = PID)**, and **pulse intensity** in real time for every ZMW.

6. **Data Output — Two Read Types:**
   - **Continuous Long Read (CLR):** Raw single-pass reads, ~10–30 kb, ~Q8–Q12 accuracy.
   - **HiFi / Circular Consensus (CCS):** The polymerase circles the SMRTbell multiple times; a consensus of N subreads gives accuracy scaling as $Q \approx -10 \log_{10}(\epsilon \cdot 1/\sqrt{N})$. Typical HiFi (N = 10–15) reaches **Q30+ accuracy (>99.9%)** with read lengths of 10–25 kb.

**(b) Advantages and Challenges (7 marks)**

**Advantages:**

1. **Long read lengths** (10–30 kb CLR, 10–25 kb HiFi) — spans repetitive regions, resolves structural variants and haplotypes.
2. **High consensus accuracy** (HiFi Q30+) — rivals short-read Illumina quality with longer context.
3. **No PCR amplification** — eliminates amplification bias and GC bias.
4. **Direct detection of base modifications** — kinetic information (PID, pulse width) reveals 5mC, 6mA, N6-methyladenosine without bisulfite conversion.
5. **Uniform coverage** — each molecule is independent; no clonal artifacts.
6. **Random errors** — errors are random and easily corrected via CCS, unlike systematic errors.
7. **High scalability** — Revio system delivers >500 Gb HiFi per SMRT Cell × 4 cells per run.

**Challenges:**

1. **High instrument cost** — Sequel IIe and Revio systems are expensive (>$1M USD).
2. **Lower raw per-pass accuracy** — CLR reads have ~85–90% per-base accuracy, requiring CCS computation.
3. **High DNA input requirement** — typically several micrograms of HMW DNA.
4. **SMRT Cell consumable cost** — $700–$1000 per cell.
5. **Computationally intensive** — HiFi calling requires significant CPU/GPU resources and BAM-level processing.
6. **Insert size bias** — smaller fragments are preferentially loaded into ZMWs.
7. **Throughput lower than Illumina** — Revio (~500 Gb) is much less than Illumina NovaSeq X (Tb-scale).

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP PEBBT755]`

**Q: Compare the principles, working mechanisms, advantages, and challenges of Oxford Nanopore sequencing and Helicos HeliScope sequencing technologies.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principles and Working Mechanisms (7 marks)**

**Oxford Nanopore (ONT):**

- **Principle:** Measures modulation of **ionic current** as a single DNA/RNA strand passes through a **biological nanopore** embedded in a polymer membrane.
- **Workflow:**
  1. Library prep: end repair, dA tailing, motor protein + adapter ligation.
  2. Flow cell contains ~512 channels × 4 pores = ~**2048 nanopores** (MinION).
  3. A voltage of **+180 to +300 mV** drives negatively charged DNA through the pore.
  4. A **motor protein** (e.g., E. coli WGA coupling) ratchets DNA at controlled speed (~400–450 bases/sec).
  5. As each 5–6 base **k-mer** occupies the pore's sensing region, the ionic current is reduced by a characteristic amount.
  6. The **squiggle signal** is recorded at ~4 kHz sampling rate.
  7. Modern **neural network basecallers** (Dorado, Guppy) translate current traces into FASTQ basecalls in real time.
- **Output:** FASTQ + POD5/FAST5 raw signal files; reads from 1 kb up to **>2 Mb ultra-long reads**.

**Helicos HeliScope (tSMS):**

- **Principle:** First **true single-molecule, non-amplification** sequencing by synthesis using **fluorescent virtual terminators** detected by **Total Internal Reflection Fluorescence (TIRF) microscopy**.
- **Workflow:**
  1. DNA fragmented to **100–200 bp**.
  2. **Poly-A tail** added to 3' ends using terminal transferase.
  3. Fragments hybridize to **dT50 oligomers** covalently attached to glass flow cell (25 channels, ~50 million capture sites/channel).
  4. One of four **fluorescently labeled virtual terminators** (Cy5, Cy3, Alexa-555, Alexa-546) + DNA polymerase flowed in.
  5. A single base is incorporated per fragment per cycle.
  6. TIRF microscopy excites the surface-bound fragments and images all positions simultaneously.
  7. **TCEP** cleaves the dye and terminator group.
  8. Cycle repeats — **~120 cycles/day** for an 8-day run.
- **Output:** 25–55 bp reads at ~Q17 accuracy; ~21–35 Gb per run.

**(b) Comparative Advantages and Challenges (7 marks)**

| Aspect | Oxford Nanopore | Helicos HeliScope |
|--------|----------------|--------------------|
| **Read length** | 1 kb to >2 Mb (ultra-long) | 25–55 bp (short!) |
| **Throughput** | 50 Gb (MinION) to ~290 Gb (PromethION) | ~21–35 Gb |
| **Error rate** | ~Q20 raw; Q30+ with consensus | ~Q17 (97%) |
| **PCR-free** | Yes | Yes |
| **Real-time** | Yes (live basecalling) | No |
| **Portable** | Yes (MinION = 90 g) | No (large benchtop) |
| **Base modification detection** | Yes (native signal) | No |
| **Field deployable** | Yes (e.g., Ebola, COVID surveillance) | No |
| **Cost per Gb** | Low (~$1–$10) | N/A (defunct) |

**Helicos Advantages (Historical):**
- **First** truly amplification-free platform.
- No GC bias, no amplification artifacts.
- Demonstrated feasibility of single-molecule sequencing for the field.

**Helicos Challenges:**
- Very **short reads (25–55 bp)** — unsuitable for assembly, isoform analysis.
- Slow throughput per run; lengthy run times.
- **Commercially defunct** since 2012; no longer available.
- High reagent cost relative to output.

**ONT Advantages (Modern):**
- Ultra-long reads enable **T2T genome assemblies**.
- Real-time, portable sequencing — revolutionised pathogen surveillance.
- Direct RNA and direct methylation detection.
- No theoretical read length limit.

**ONT Challenges:**
- Higher per-base error rate than Illumina or PacBio HiFi (mitigated by newer pores + basecallers).
- High-quality HMW DNA required for ultra-long protocols.
- Signal noise and basecall accuracy depend on chemistry version and basecaller updates.
- Pore degradation over time reduces yield late in the run.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PEBBT755 Examinations:**
> 
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Helicos' "single-molecule" status with long reads.** Helicos reads were **25–55 bp** — shorter than even early Illumina reads! The "single-molecule" descriptor refers to **lack of amplification**, not read length.
> 
> 2. **ONT reads are NOT electrical basecalling directly.** Students often write "ONT measures nucleotide charge." Wrong! It measures **modulation of ionic current** through the electrolyte; the bases themselves are read indirectly by their k-mer-specific current signature.
> 
> 3. **PacBio errors are INDEPENDENT, while ONT homopolymer errors are systematic.** This is why PacBio's CCS gives Q30+ easily, but ONT requires more sophisticated consensus (duplex) protocols.
> 
> 4. **ZMW = Zero-Mode Waveguide, not "Zero-Mode Well"** — examiners deduct marks for this terminological error.
> 
> 5. **SMRT = Single Molecule Real-Time** — do not write "small molecule real-time" or "single molecule reverse transcription"!
> 
> 6. **Helicos Bankrupt in 2012** — examiners expect students to know the historical context and the reason for failure (technical limitations, competition from Illumina).
> 
> 7. **HiFi is NOT a separate platform** — it is a *consensus* mode within PacBio using CCS reads from the Sequel II/IIe/Revio systems.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Helicos HeliScope (tSMS)** — first single-molecule platform (2008); **poly-A + dT50 capture; TIRF fluorescence; virtual terminators; TCEP cleavage; 25–55 bp reads; ~Q17; DEFUNCT since 2012**.
- [ ] **Oxford Nanopore (ONT)** — **electrical detection** of ionic current through biological nanopores; **~180–300 mV**; motor protein at ~400 bps; **modern basecaller = Dorado neural network**; reads **>2 Mb** (ultra-long); real-time; portable MinION.
- [ ] **PacBio SMRT** — **Zero-Mode Waveguides (ZMWs) ~70 nm; ~20 zL observation volume; evanescent wave decay; phospholink chemistry; SMRTbell circular template**; CLR (long, Q10) vs HiFi (Q30+ consensus); 10–25 kb typical read.
- [ ] All three are **PCR-free, single-molecule, third-generation** platforms.
- [ ] **Read length hierarchy:** Helicos (25–55 bp) << PacBio (10–30 kb) << ONT ultra-long (>2 Mb).
- [ ] **Accuracy hierarchy (raw, single-pass):** ONT CLR ~Q10 ≈ PacBio CLR ~Q8–Q12 < PacBio HiFi Q30+ (CCS consensus).
- [ ] **Base modification detection:** PacBio (kinetics/PID), ONT (signal-based); Helicos = **no** base-mod detection.
- [ ] **PacBio CCS formula** (essential): $Q_{consensus} \approx -10 \cdot \log_{10}(\epsilon) + 10 \cdot \log_{10}(\sqrt{N})$; for $\epsilon = 0.10$ and $N = 10$, Q ≈ 28; for $N = 15$, Q ≈ 30+.
- [ ] **ONT current formula:** $I = V / R_{pore}$; residual current modulation encodes k-mer identity.
- [ ] **ZMW physics:** Light decay $I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z/\lambda_{evanescent}}$ with $\lambda_{evanescent} \approx 20$ nm.
- [ ] **PacBio Sequel II = 8M ZMWs; Revio = 4 SMRT Cells × higher density**.
- [ ] **MinION = 2048 nanopores (512 channels × 4); PromethION = 48 flow cells × thousands of pores each**.
- [ ] **Library prep — PacBio = SMRTbell; ONT = LSK (ligation) or Rapid (transposase)**.
- [ ] **Direct RNA sequencing is currently unique to ONT** (PacBio Iso-Seq uses cDNA, though it is full-length).
- [ ] **PacBio T2T + ONT T2T** — both contributed to the complete human genome project (T2T-CHM13, 2022).
- [ ] **Helicos 2012 bankruptcy** — caused by high cost-per-Gb, short reads, and competition with rapidly improving Illumina platforms.
- [ ] **Modern PacBio + ONT can match Illumina accuracy** while providing long-range information.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 weightage:** Expect 1 Part A (3 marks) + 1 Part B (14 marks) sub-question from this topic per exam; focus on principles, working, and challenges.
- [ ] **Output file formats:** PacBio = BAM/CCS; ONT = FAST5 or POD5 + FASTQ; Helicos = QSEQ (legacy) → FASTQ.
- [ ] **Q-score interpretation:** Q20 = 1% error, Q30 = 0.1% error, Q40 = 0.01% error.
- [ ] **Industry cost trends:** ONT is currently the cheapest long-read platform per Gb, followed by PacBio; both have dropped dramatically since 2015.
- [ ] **Single-molecule detection does NOT imply "100% accuracy"** — all single-molecule platforms have characteristic error profiles that require consensus or basecalling improvements to overcome.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
