topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT755 - NEXT GENERATION SEQUENCING TECHNOLOGIES: DATA ANALYSIS AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of the Three Generations of Sequencing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Sequencing" in Molecular Biology?

**Sequencing** is the biochemical process of determining the precise order of nucleotides — **Adenine (A)**, **Thymine (T)**, **Cytosine (C)**, and **Guanine (G)** — within a DNA or RNA molecule. In the context of KTU's PEBBT755 syllabus, sequencing refers to the *technological platforms* that automate this reading process at scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A *generation* of sequencing technology is defined by its **underlying biochemistry**, **read length capability**, **throughput (bases per run)**, and **degree of parallelism**. Three distinct generations are recognized: **First Generation (Sanger)**, **Second Generation (Massively Parallel / NGS)**, and **Third Generation (Single-Molecule, Long-Read)**.

## 1.2 The Three Generations — Intuitive Overview

### 🔬 First Generation: Sanger Sequencing (1977 – mid 2000s)
**Analogy — "Reading a Book by Shining a Flashlight on One Page at a Time":**
Imagine you have a 1,000-page book, but you are only allowed to read one page at a time using a flashlight. You can read it *perfectly accurately*, but it will take a very long time. Sanger sequencing works exactly like this: it reads one short DNA fragment (typically **500–1000 bases**) with extremely high accuracy (**>99.99%**), but only one fragment at a time.

### 🧬 Second Generation: Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) — (mid 2000s – present)
**Analogy — "A Million People Simultaneously Reading Random Shreds of the Same Book":**
Now imagine taking that same 1,000-page book, shredding it into millions of tiny pieces, and giving each piece to a different person to read at the same time. The reading is fast and cheap *per page*, but each person only sees a 100–300 character snippet. To reconstruct the book, you must computationally piece the fragments back together. This is *short-read massively parallel sequencing* — the workhorse of modern genomics.

### 🧠 Third Generation: Single-Molecule Long-Read Sequencing (2010s – present)
**Analogy — "Reading Whole Chapters in One Long Glance Without Shattering the Book":**
In this generation, you don't shred the book. Instead, you read *entire chapters* (often **10,000–1,000,000 bases** in a single read) by watching a single DNA molecule as it passes through a detector. No amplification, no fragmentation, no assembly puzzle — just one continuous, long read.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The transition from Generation 1 → 2 → 3 is primarily driven by three engineering goals: **(i) increase throughput**, **(ii) reduce cost per base**, and **(iii) increase read length while preserving single-base accuracy.**

## 1.3 Historical Context (KTU Board-Favorite Timeline)

| Year | Milestone | Generation |
|------|-----------|------------|
| **1977** | Sanger chain-termination method published | **1st** |
| **1986** | First automated DNA sequencer (ABI 370) | **1st** |
| **1990–2003** | Human Genome Project completed (~$3 billion) | **1st** |
| **2005** | 454 Roche pyrosequencing launched | **2nd** |
| **2006** | Solexa/Illumina platform introduced | **2nd** |
| **2010** | Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) SMRT released | **3rd** |
| **2012** | Oxford Nanopore MinION released | **3rd** |
| **2022** | T2T (Telomere-to-Telomere) human genome completed | **3rd** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Telomere-to-Telomere (T2T) Consortium** finally published a *gapless* human genome in 2022 using long-read PacBio HiFi + Oxford Nanopore technology — a milestone impossible with short reads alone.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Metrics Sheet

## 2.1 First Generation — Sanger Chain-Termination Sequencing

### Core Biochemistry
Sanger sequencing exploits **dideoxynucleotides (ddNTPs)**. Unlike normal **deoxynucleotides (dNTPs)**, ddNTPs lack a 3'-OH group. When a DNA polymerase incorporates a ddNTP, **no further phosphodiester bond can form**, terminating the chain.

**The four parallel reactions** each contain:
- A DNA template
- A primer
- DNA polymerase
- All four normal dNTPs
- A *small amount* of one labeled ddNTP (ddATP, ddTTP, ddCGP, or ddGTP)

The result: a population of DNA fragments of *every possible length*, each ending at a specific base. These are then separated by **capillary electrophoresis**, and the terminal base is identified by a fluorescent label.

### Operational Steps (KTU 14-Mark Format)

1. **Template preparation:** Clone DNA fragment into a plasmid vector (M13mp) or PCR-amplify the target region.
2. **Cycle sequencing:** Four tubes (or one tube with four differently labeled ddNTPs in modern capillary systems) undergo primer extension.
3. **Chain termination:** Random incorporation of fluorescent ddNTPs halts elongation at every possible position.
4. **Size separation:** Capillary electrophoresis separates fragments down to **single-base resolution**.
5. **Detection:** A laser excites the fluorophore; an electropherogram is generated showing peaks of A, T, C, G.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Board Fact:** Modern Sanger sequencers (e.g., ABI 3730xl) use a **single tube** with four different fluorophores, achieving read lengths of up to **~1000 bases** with **Q20+ phred scores** (1 error in 100 bases = 99% accuracy).

### Limitations
- Low throughput: only **~96 samples per run**
- Cost per base is *high* (~$0.50 per finished base in the 1990s)
- Cannot handle complex genomes with repetitive regions

## 2.2 Second Generation — Massively Parallel Sequencing (NGS)

The hallmark of all 2nd-generation platforms is the **clonal amplification** of single DNA molecules before sequencing, enabling millions of parallel reactions.

### Three Dominant 2nd-Generation Chemistries

| Platform | Chemistry | Detection | Read Length |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Illumina (Solexa)** | Sequencing By Synthesis (SBS), reversible terminators | Fluorescent imaging | 50–600 bp |
| **Ion Torrent** | SBS + H⁺ ion detection | pH change (ISFET) | 200–600 bp |
| **454 Roche (discontinued)** | Pyrosequencing (luciferase light) | Chemiluminescence | ~700 bp |
| **SOLiD (discontinued)** | Sequencing By Ligation (SBL) | Two-base encoding | 50–75 bp |

### Illumina Workflow (Most Widely Used — KTU Board Favorite)
1. **Library prep:** Fragment DNA to **~300–500 bp**; ligate sequencing adapters.
2. **Cluster generation (bridge amplification):** Single molecules bind to a flow cell; each is amplified into a clonal cluster of ~1,000 copies via bridge PCR.
3. **Sequencing by synthesis:** Incorporate one fluorescently-labeled reversible terminator per cycle. Image all clusters. Cleave the terminator. Repeat.
4. **Base calling:** Identify A, T, C, G from cluster fluorescence intensities.
5. **Output:** Hundreds of millions of short reads per run (e.g., NovaSeq 6000: **~6 Tb / 2×150 bp run**).

### Pyrosequencing (Roche 454) — Historical Significance
When a polymerase incorporates a dNTP, **pyrophosphate (PPi)** is released. PPi is converted to **ATP by ATP sulfurylase**, which drives **luciferase to produce light**. Each light flash = one base incorporated. The signal intensity is *proportional* to the number of bases in a homopolymer stretch (e.g., "AAA" → triple the light of "A").

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Pyrosequencing struggles to accurately quantify long homopolymers (e.g., AAAAAA = 6 A's). This is why Roche 454 was eventually discontinued.

## 2.3 Third Generation — Single-Molecule, Long-Read Sequencing

### Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) — SMRT Sequencing
- **Zero-Mode Waveguides (ZMWs):** Tiny wells (~70 nm diameter) hold a single DNA polymerase molecule anchored at the bottom.
- **SMRTbell template:** A circular DNA molecule is continuously read; the polymerase can go around the circle **multiple times** to generate **Circular Consensus Reads (CCRs)** → ultra-high accuracy (**Q50, 99.999%**).
- **Read length:** 10,000–25,000 bp routinely; HiFi reads achieve **~99.9% accuracy**.

### Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT)
- **Nanopore principle:** A single strand of DNA is fed through a **biological nanopore** (e.g., α-hemolysin) embedded in a membrane.
- **Signal:** As each k-mer of ~6 bases occupies the pore, it disrupts an applied ionic current in a *base-specific* way.
- **Base calling:** A neural network (typically a recurrent or transformer architecture) decodes the current squiggle into nucleotide sequence.
- **Read length:** **>1,000,000 bp** possible (theoretically limited only by DNA molecule length).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept:** Both PacBio SMRT and ONT detect *single molecules* — **no PCR amplification step is required**. This is the defining philosophical shift of the third generation.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Metrics Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Sanger (1st) | Illumina NGS (2nd) | PacBio / ONT (3rd) |
|--------|-------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Read Length** | 500–1000 bp | 50–600 bp | 10,000 – >1,000,000 bp |
| **Throughput per run** | ~96 kb | 100 Gb – 6 Tb | 30 Gb – 500 Gb |
| **Accuracy (Q-score)** | Q30–Q40 (99.9–99.99%) | Q30 (99.9%) | Q20–Q50 (PacBio HiFi Q30) |
| **Cost per Mb (USD, ~2023)** | ~$500 | ~$0.01 | ~$0.10–$0.50 |
| **Sample prep time** | 1 day | 1–3 days | 1–2 days |
| **Amplification needed?** | Yes (PCR) | Yes (bridge PCR) | **No (single molecule)** |
| **Best suited for** | Small amplicons, validation | Whole-genome resequencing, RNA-seq, ChIP-seq | De novo assembly, structural variants, T2T genomes |
| **GC bias / coverage uniformity** | High uniformity | Strong GC bias | More uniform |

### The Three KTU "Big Equations" You Must Know

**Equation 1 — Sequencing Coverage (Depth):**
$$ \text{Coverage} = \frac{L \times N}{G} $$

where $L$ = average read length (bp), $N$ = number of reads, $G$ = haploid genome size (bp).

**Equation 2 — Phred Quality Score:**
$$ Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{error}}) $$

So **Q20** = 1% error (99% accuracy), **Q30** = 0.1% error (99.9% accuracy), **Q50** = 0.001% error.

**Equation 3 — Number of Reads Required for Target Coverage:**
$$ N = \frac{C \times G}{L} $$

where $C$ = desired coverage depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Coverage depth determines statistical confidence in variant calling. KTU Board examiners love asking: *"How many 150 bp Illumina reads are needed for 30× coverage of the human genome (3 Gb)?"* — The answer is computed directly from Equation 3.

## 2.5 Comparative Trade-off: The "Sequencing Triangle"

Every sequencing technology trades off between three competing engineering constraints:

- **Read length** ↕
- **Throughput** ↕
- **Per-base accuracy** ↕

Sanger optimized accuracy at the cost of throughput. NGS optimized throughput at the cost of read length. Third-generation platforms have *finally* broken this trade-off, simultaneously delivering long reads *and* high accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 — Coverage Depth Calculation (KTU 3-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A researcher uses an Illumina sequencer to sequence the *E. coli* genome. The output consists of **4,000,000 reads**, each of length **150 bp**. The *E. coli* haploid genome is **4.6 Mbp (4,600,000 bp)**. Compute the average coverage depth.

### Step 1 — Identify Known Variables
- Number of reads: $N = 4{,}000{,}000$
- Read length: $L = 150 \text{ bp}$
- Genome size: $G = 4{,}600{,}000 \text{ bp}$

### Step 2 — Substitute Into the Coverage Formula
$$ \text{Coverage} = \frac{L \times N}{G} $$

### Step 3 — Evaluate Numerator
$$ L \times N = 150 \times 4{,}000{,}000 = 600{,}000{,}000 \text{ bp} $$

### Step 4 — Divide by Genome Size
$$ \text{Coverage} = \frac{600{,}000{,}000}{4{,}600{,}000} $$

### Step 5 — Simplify
$$ \text{Coverage} \approx 130.4 \times $$

**Answer:** The *E. coli* genome is sequenced at **~130× average coverage**, far above the typical 30× minimum needed for confident variant calling.

## 3.2 Example 2 — Phred Score to Error Probability (KTU 3-Mark)

**Problem:** A sequencing platform reports an average base quality of **Q40**. What is the probability that any given base is *incorrectly* called?

### Step 1 — Start with the Phred Equation
$$ Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{error}}) $$

### Step 2 — Rearrange to Solve for $P_{\text{error}}$
$$ \log_{10}(P_{\text{error}}) = -\frac{Q}{10} $$

$$ P_{\text{error}} = 10^{-Q/10} $$

### Step 3 — Substitute $Q = 40$
$$ P_{\text{error}} = 10^{-40/10} = 10^{-4} $$

### Step 4 — Convert to Percentage
$$ P_{\text{error}} = 0.0001 = 0.01\% $$

**Answer:** A **Q40** base has only a **0.01%** chance of being wrong, equivalent to **1 error in 10,000 bases** — a hallmark of Sanger / PacBio HiFi accuracy.

## 3.3 Example 3 — Read Count Required for Target Coverage (KTU 14-Mark Variant)

**Problem:** You wish to sequence the human genome (**3,000 Mb = 3 × 10⁹ bp**) at **30× coverage** using an Illumina platform with read length **100 bp (paired-end; count one read as 100 bp)**. How many reads are required?

### Step 1 — Identify Variables
- Target coverage: $C = 30$
- Genome size: $G = 3 \times 10^9 \text{ bp}$
- Read length: $L = 100 \text{ bp}$

### Step 2 — Apply the Rearranged Coverage Formula
$$ N = \frac{C \times G}{L} $$

### Step 3 — Substitute
$$ N = \frac{30 \times 3 \times 10^9}{100} $$

### Step 4 — Compute Numerator
$$ 30 \times 3 \times 10^9 = 9 \times 10^{10} $$

### Step 5 — Divide
$$ N = \frac{9 \times 10^{10}}{100} = 9 \times 10^8 $$

**Answer:** Approximately **900 million reads** are needed — which is why modern Illumina NovaSeq flow cells can multiplex many genomes in a single run (multiplexing is a downstream cost-saving step).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If the question states *paired-end* sequencing with fragment length $2L$, treat each *pair* as generating **2L bases of sequence data**. Adjust $L$ in the formula accordingly.

## 3.4 Worked Walkthrough: The Illumina Bridge Amplification Cycle

The KTU Board frequently asks for a labelled diagram *or* a step-by-step description of Illumina cluster generation. Here is the exhaustive 4-step cycle:

1. **Denaturation:** Double-stranded library fragments are melted into single strands.
2. **Annealing to P5/P7 oligos:** Single-stranded DNA binds to complementary oligos grafted onto the flow cell surface.
3. **Extension:** DNA polymerase extends the oligo, creating a *bridge* structure.
4. **Denaturation again:** The bridge is melted; one strand remains anchored, the other swings free and bends over to bind a *neighboring* oligo of the opposite type.

This cycle repeats **~30–35 times**, producing a **clonal cluster of ~1,000 identical copies** at one physical location. The reverse strands are then cleaved to leave only forward strands ready for sequencing-by-synthesis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why amplification?** A single DNA molecule is too dim to image. By clustering, the fluorescence signal is amplified into a detectable "dot" on the flow cell — this is the core optical trick that makes Illumina work.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Generation" in the context of DNA sequencing technologies. List the three generations and give one representative platform for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A "generation" in DNA sequencing refers to a distinct class of sequencing technology defined by its underlying biochemistry, read length, throughput, and degree of parallelism. The three generations are:
1. **First Generation** — Sanger chain-termination method (1977). Representative platform: ABI 3730xl capillary sequencer.
2. **Second Generation** — Massively parallel short-read sequencing (mid-2000s onward). Representative platform: Illumina NovaSeq / NextSeq.
3. **Third Generation** — Single-molecule, long-read sequencing (2010+). Representative platforms: Pacific Biosciences Sequel II and Oxford Nanopore MinION / PromethION.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a "Phred quality score" of Q30, and what does it imply about base-call accuracy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Phred quality score is a logarithmic measure of base-calling accuracy, defined by $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{error}})$.
- For **Q30**: $P_{\text{error}} = 10^{-3} = 0.001 = 0.1\%$.
- This implies a **99.9% probability that the called base is correct**, i.e., 1 error in 1,000 bases. Q30 is the de facto industry standard for variant calling confidence in Illumina sequencing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **biochemistry and workflow of Sanger's chain-termination method** of DNA sequencing. Clearly explain the role of ddNTPs, the function of capillary electrophoresis, and how the final nucleotide sequence is determined. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **First Generation (Sanger) sequencing with Second Generation (Illumina NGS)** across the following axes: read length, throughput, cost per base, amplification requirement, and a suitable application for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Sanger Biochemistry and Workflow (7 marks)

1. **Principle (1 mark):** Sanger sequencing uses **dideoxynucleotides (ddNTPs)** that lack a 3'-OH group. When incorporated by DNA polymerase, they cause *irreversible chain termination* because no phosphodiester bond can form with the next nucleotide.

2. **Reaction Setup (2 marks):**
   - A single-stranded DNA template is mixed with a primer, DNA polymerase, all four normal dNTPs, and a *small fraction* of one of the four ddNTPs (each labeled with a distinct fluorophore in modern single-tube systems).
   - The result is a population of fragments of **every possible length**, each terminating at a different position of the target base.

3. **Size Separation (2 marks):**
   - Capillary electrophoresis separates the fragments at **single-base resolution** based on length. Shorter fragments migrate faster.
   - As fragments exit the capillary, a laser excites the fluorophore on the terminal ddNTP.

4. **Detection and Readout (2 marks):**
   - A fluorescence detector records the color (A, T, C, or G) of each passing fragment.
   - The electropherogram is read in order of fragment size from smallest to largest, reconstructing the original template sequence.
   - Read length achievable: up to **~1000 bases** with >99.99% accuracy.

#### Model Solution (b) — Comparison Table + Application (7 marks)

| Feature | Sanger (1st Gen) | Illumina NGS (2nd Gen) |
|---------|------------------|------------------------|
| Read length | 500–1000 bp | 50–600 bp (commonly 2×150) |
| Throughput per run | ~96 kb | 100 Gb – 6 Tb |
| Cost per base | ~$0.50 | ~$0.01 (10,000× cheaper) |
| Amplification | Required (PCR/plasmid) | Required (bridge amplification) |
| Accuracy | Q40+ (99.99%) | Q30 (99.9%) |
| Best application | Validating cloned inserts, targeted amplicons, clinical mutation confirmation | Whole-genome resequencing, RNA-seq, ChIP-seq, metagenomics, large-scale variant discovery |

**Application commentary (1 mark):** Sanger remains the gold standard for *low-throughput, high-confidence* clinical variant validation; Illumina dominates *high-throughput* population-scale studies.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of **Illumina Sequencing-By-Synthesis (SBS)**. Describe the roles of **bridge amplification**, **reversible terminators**, and **fluorescence imaging** in generating short reads. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **two third-generation single-molecule sequencing platforms** in detail. Explain how each platform detects nucleotides *without prior amplification* and discuss one major advantage of long-read technology over short-read NGS. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Illumina SBS Principle (7 marks)

1. **Library preparation (1 mark):** Genomic DNA is fragmented to ~300–500 bp, end-repaired, and ligated to platform-specific **adapter oligos**.

2. **Bridge amplification (2 marks):**
   - Adapter-ligated fragments bind to complementary **P5/P7 oligos** grafted onto the flow cell surface.
   - Polymerase extends the bound strand; it bends over and binds a *neighboring* oligo, forming a "bridge."
   - Repeated denaturation + extension cycles produce **clonal clusters of ~1,000 identical copies** at each coordinate.

3. **Sequencing-by-synthesis with reversible terminators (2 marks):**
   - Each cycle adds **one** fluorescently-labeled dNTP whose 3'-OH is chemically *blocked* (reversible terminator). Only **one base is incorporated per cycle**, ensuring perfect synchrony.
   - After imaging, the fluorophore and the 3'-blocking group are **chemically cleaved**, restoring the 3'-OH for the next cycle.

4. **Fluorescence imaging and base-calling (2 marks):**
   - A high-resolution camera images **all clusters simultaneously** in four channels.
   - The intensity at each cluster identifies which base (A, T, C, or G) was incorporated. After ~150–600 cycles, hundreds of millions of short reads are output as FASTQ files.

#### Model Solution (b) — Two Third-Generation Platforms (7 marks)

**Platform 1 — Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) SMRT (3.5 marks):**
- Single DNA polymerase molecules are anchored at the bottom of **Zero-Mode Waveguides (ZMWs)** — nanophotonic wells that isolate single molecules from bulk excitation light.
- A **circular SMRTbell template** is continuously synthesized; the polymerase can traverse the circle **multiple times**, generating **Circular Consensus Reads (CCRs)** that yield **Q30+ accuracy** even though individual passes are ~Q10.
- Real-time fluorescence is recorded as labeled dNTPs are incorporated — each of the four dNTPs has a distinct fluorophore attached to its terminal phosphate, which is cleaved during incorporation.
- **No amplification required** — single-molecule detection.

**Platform 2 — Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT) (3.5 marks):**
- A single strand of DNA is threaded through a **biological nanopore** (e.g., engineered α-hemolysin) embedded in an electrically resistive membrane.
- An applied voltage drives a steady ionic current through the pore. As a **6-mer (6-base k-mer) of DNA occupies the pore**, it partially blocks the current in a *base-specific* manner.
- The resulting time-series current trace ("squiggle") is decoded by a **neural network basecaller** (typically a transformer model) into nucleotide sequence.
- Reads can exceed **1,000,000 bp** because there is no enzymatic processivity limit imposed by polymerase kinetics — only the physical length of the input DNA.

**Major advantage of long reads over short reads (1 mark):** Long reads can span **repetitive regions** and **structural variants** (translocations, large insertions, inversions) that are *inaccessible* to short-read assembly, enabling *de novo* assembly of complex genomes — culminating in the gapless T2T human genome (2022).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the generations:** Students often mix up "second generation" with "third generation." Remember: **amplification = 2nd gen**; **single molecule = 3rd gen**.
> 2. **Wrong formula application:** In coverage questions, do *not* forget to multiply by read length. Many students write $C = N/G$ instead of $C = LN/G$, losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the 'why':** A KTU Board examiner will not award full marks for a *definition alone* — you must explain the *underlying biochemistry* (e.g., why ddNTPs terminate, why reversible terminators synchronize).
> 4. **Phred confusion:** $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$ is *not* $Q = 10 \log_{10}(P)$. Sign matters. Q30 = 99.9% accuracy, not 0.1% accuracy.
> 5. **Forgetting internal choice:** KTU 14-mark questions come as **either-or**; pick the one you can answer *most thoroughly*. Do not attempt both.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A sequencing "**generation**" is defined by **chemistry, throughput, and read length**.
- ✅ **1st Generation (Sanger):** ddNTP chain termination + capillary electrophoresis; read length ~1000 bp; Q40+ accuracy; gold standard for validation.
- ✅ **2nd Generation (NGS / Illumina):** Bridge amplification + reversible terminator SBS; reads 50–600 bp; ~Q30 accuracy; massive parallelism; workhorse of modern genomics.
- ✅ **2nd Gen also includes 454 pyrosequencing** (light via luciferase), **Ion Torrent** (pH change), and **SOLiD** (two-base ligation) — but Illumina dominates.
- ✅ **3rd Generation (PacBio + ONT):** Single-molecule detection, **no amplification**, reads 10,000 bp to >1,000,000 bp.
- ✅ **PacBio SMRT:** Zero-mode waveguides + circular consensus → high accuracy long reads.
- ✅ **Oxford Nanopore:** Ionic current disruption through biological nanopore → potentially ultra-long reads.
- ✅ **Coverage formula:** $C = LN / G$. **Phred score:** $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{error}})$.
- ✅ Q20 = 99% accuracy, **Q30 = 99.9% accuracy**, Q40 = 99.99% accuracy.
- ✅ Long reads can resolve **repetitive regions, structural variants, and enable *de novo* genome assembly** — culminating in the **T2T gapless human genome (2022)**.
- ✅ **Sanger → NGS → Long-read** evolution follows the engineering goals of **higher throughput, lower cost per base, and longer reads**.
- ✅ The **sequencing triangle trade-off** (read length ↔ throughput ↔ accuracy) was finally broken by 3rd-gen platforms, which now deliver **all three simultaneously**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enrichment Libraries II: ChIP and ATAC Sequencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT755 - NEXT GENERATION SEQUENCING TECHNOLOGIES: DATA ANALYSIS AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Enrichment Libraries II: ChIP and ATAC Sequencing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Enrichment Libraries** in Next Generation Sequencing (NGS) refer to specialized sequencing library preparation strategies that selectively capture and amplify genomic regions of interest (e.g., protein-bound DNA, open chromatin) prior to high-throughput sequencing, thereby dramatically reducing sequencing cost and increasing biological signal-to-noise ratio.

### 1.1 ChIP Sequencing (ChIP-seq)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
Chromatin Immunoprecipitation followed by Sequencing (**ChIP-seq**) is a high-throughput enrichment library method used to identify the genome-wide binding sites of DNA-associated proteins (such as transcription factors, histone modifiers, or chromatin remodelers) and to profile specific histone post-translational modifications (e.g., H3K4me3, H3K27ac). The technique couples *in vivo* protein-DNA crosslinking, antibody-based immunoprecipitation, and massively parallel sequencing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ChIP-seq is the gold standard for mapping *in vivo* protein–DNA interactions at a genome-wide scale.** First published in 2007 (Johnson et al. and Barski et al.), it remains foundational in epigenomics, cancer biology, and gene regulation studies under the KTU 2024 bioinformatics curriculum.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Fishing Net" Model:**
Imagine a fast-flowing river representing the entire genome. Specific fish (transcription factor proteins) are swimming attached to particular stones (DNA binding sites). To find where these fish are concentrated:
1. You throw a net that grabs the fish along with the stones they are holding (**crosslinking**).
2. You use a highly specific hook (the **antibody**) that catches only the fish you are interested in (**immunoprecipitation**).
3. You then read the labels on the stones to know exactly where each fish was swimming (**sequencing the bound DNA fragments**).
The stones with no fish attached (unbound DNA) are washed away — hence, **enrichment**.

### 1.2 ATAC Sequencing (ATAC-seq)

**Formal Definition:**
**ATAC-seq (Assay for Transposase-Accessible Chromatin using sequencing)** is a sensitive, fast, and low-input enrichment library method that uses a hyperactive Tn5 transposase to simultaneously fragment open chromatin and insert sequencing adapters into accessible genomic regions. The read density reflects local chromatin accessibility, nucleosome positioning, and transcription factor occupancy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ATAC-seq is sometimes called the "ChIP-seq for chromatin openness."** It was introduced by Buenrostro et al. (2013) and has rapidly become the method of choice for epigenomic profiling in limited cell numbers, including single cells.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Skeleton Key" Model:**
Consider the genome as a long building with thousands of locked doors. A special key — the **Tn5 transposase** — is engineered to try every door:
- **Open doors** (euchromatin, active regulatory regions, nucleosome-depleted regions) → the key enters, cuts the DNA, and inserts sequencing adapters at that spot.
- **Locked doors** (heterochromatin, nucleosome-occupied DNA) → the key simply cannot insert.
After sequencing, locations with many inserted adapters are flagged as **accessible chromatin**. It is like leaving a paint mark only on doors the key could open — later, the paint marks reveal the building's floor plan of "active rooms."

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used in These Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Sequencing & Library Standards:**
> - **Crosslinking reagent:** Formaldehyde (1% final concentration, ~10 minutes at room temperature).
> - **Sonication fragment size target:** 200–500 bp.
> - **Tn5 transposase loading concentration (ATAC):** 100 nM stock; 2.5 µL per 50 µL reaction.
> - **Standard sequencing read length:** 50–150 bp paired-end.
> - **Recommended sequencing depth:** 20–60 million reads (ChIP-seq); 30–100 million reads (ATAC-seq).
> - **Reference genome assembly (KTU 2024 standard):** GRCh38 (human) or mm10 (mouse).
> - **GC content of enriched libraries:** Typically 40–60%.
> - **Insert size distribution for ATAC-seq:** Sub-nucleosomal (~100 bp), mono-nucleosomal (~200 bp), di-nucleosomal (~400 bp) peaks.

### 1.4 Why Enrichment Libraries Matter in NGS

Both ChIP-seq and ATAC-seq belong to the **enrichment library** family because they do *not* sequence the entire genome uniformly. Instead, they:

1. **Pre-select** biologically meaningful regions (bound DNA or open chromatin).
2. **Reduce sequencing cost** by 10–100× compared to whole-genome sequencing.
3. **Increase statistical power** by concentrating reads in regions of interest.
4. **Enable hypothesis-driven interrogation** of specific molecular events.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ChIP-seq — Operational Logic in 7 Steps

1. **Crosslinking:** Cells are treated with **formaldehyde (1% v/v)** to covalently link proteins to their bound DNA. The reaction is quenched with **glycine (125 mM)**.
   - *Why:* Captures transient and stable protein–DNA interactions *in situ*.
2. **Cell Lysis & Chromatin Extraction:** Cells are lysed using a buffer containing protease inhibitors (PMSF, aprotinin, leupeptin). Nuclei are isolated, and chromatin is solubilized.
3. **Sonication (Chromatin Shearing):** DNA is fragmented to **200–500 bp** using high-frequency ultrasound.
   - *Why:* Required size for efficient immunoprecipitation and short-read sequencing compatibility.
4. **Immunoprecipitation (IP):** A **target-specific antibody** (e.g., anti-CTCF, anti-H3K4me3) is incubated with the sheared chromatin. Protein A/G beads capture the antibody–protein–DNA complex.
5. **Washing:** Stringent washes (low salt, high salt, LiCl, TE buffers) remove non-specific binders.
6. **Reverse Crosslinking:** Proteinase K digestion at **65°C overnight** removes proteins and reverses formaldehyde crosslinks.
7. **Library Preparation & Sequencing:** DNA is end-repaired, A-tailed, ligated to Illumina adapters, PCR-amplified, and sequenced.

### 2.2 ATAC-seq — Operational Logic in 5 Steps

1. **Cell Lysis & Nuclei Isolation:** ~50,000 viable cells are lysed in a mild non-ionic detergent (e.g., NP-40) to isolate intact nuclei.
2. **Tn5 Transposition Reaction:** Nuclei are incubated with a **pre-assembled Tn5 transposase–adapter complex** (often called "tagmented" reaction) for 30–60 minutes at 37°C.
   - *Why:* Tn5 simultaneously cuts DNA *and* ligates sequencing adapters — only in accessible regions.
3. **DNA Purification:** Qiagen MinElute or similar column purification.
4. **PCR Amplification:** Limited-cycle PCR (typically 5–12 cycles) with index primers to amplify and barcode the library.
5. **Size Selection & Sequencing:** bead-based size selection removes large fragments; paired-end sequencing (e.g., 2× 50 bp) is performed.

> The following table consolidates every critical equation, score, and statistical model used in ChIP-seq and ATAC-seq data analysis. Use this as your last-minute revision anchor.

|---|---------|---------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **MACS2 Peak Calling (Poisson Model)** | $P(X \geq k) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{\lambda^{i} e^{-\lambda}}{i!}$ | $\lambda$ = local background read count; $k$ = observed tag count | Computes p-value for enriched peaks vs. background |
| 2 | **Fold Enrichment (ChIP-seq)** | $FE = \frac{\text{Reads in Peak}_{\text{ChIP}}}{\text{Reads in Peak}_{\text{Input}}}$ | Unitless ratio | Measures IP specificity |
| 3 | **FRiP Score (Fraction of Reads in Peaks)** | $FRiP = \frac{\text{Reads overlapping peaks}}{\text{Total mapped reads}}$ | Value 0–1; ChIP-seq: $FRiP \geq 0.01$ (ENCODE standard) | Library quality benchmark |
| 4 | **TSS Enrichment Score (ATAC-seq)** | $TSS_{score} = \frac{\text{Reads in TSS} \pm 2\text{kb}}{\text{Reads in whole gene body}}$ | Fold enrichment $\geq 4$ = good ATAC library | Validates accessible chromatin capture |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Life Sciences

- **Drug Discovery:** Identifying transcription factor binding sites perturbed by candidate drugs.
- **Cancer Epigenomics:** Profiling tumor-specific open chromatin landscapes (ATAC-seq) and histone acetylation (H3K27ac ChIP-seq).
- **Developmental Biology:** Mapping enhancer activation during embryogenesis.
- **Clinical Diagnostics:** Chromatin accessibility signatures as biomarkers for disease classification.
- **Agricultural Biotechnology:** Engineering crops with optimized chromatin accessibility at yield-related loci.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: MACS2 Poisson Statistical Model for Peak Calling

The Model-based Analysis for ChIP-Seq (**MACS**) algorithm models the local background read count $\lambda$ at a genomic position using a Poisson distribution, then tests whether the observed tag count $k$ significantly exceeds $\lambda$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Prerequisite Definitions:**
> - $\lambda$ = expected number of tags in a window under the null hypothesis (background).
> - $k$ = observed tag count at a candidate peak.
> - $e$ = Euler's number $\approx 2.71828$.
> - $i!$ = factorial of $i$.

**Step 1: State the Null Hypothesis**
Under the null hypothesis (no ChIP enrichment), the number of reads in any window of size $d$ follows a Poisson distribution:

$$P(X = i \mid \lambda) = \frac{\lambda^{i} e^{-\lambda}}{i!}$$

This is read as: "The probability of observing exactly $i$ tags in a window, given the background rate $\lambda$, equals $\lambda$ raised to $i$, multiplied by $e$ to the power of $-\lambda$, all divided by $i$ factorial."

**Step 2: Compute the Local Background Lambda**
MACS shifts all ChIP reads by $d/2$ (half the sonication fragment size) toward the 3' end, then computes $\lambda$ from a user-defined window (default 1 kb or 10 kb dynamic window).

$$\lambda_{local} = \frac{\text{Reads in sliding window of size } w}{\text{Number of eligible genomic positions in } w}$$

In plain English: We slide a window of width $w$ across the genome, count how many reads fall inside, and divide by the number of non-excluded positions to get the average local background density.

**Step 3: Compute the P-value for the Observed Count**
We test the one-sided alternative hypothesis: "the observed count is *greater* than expected by chance."

$$P(X \geq k \mid \lambda) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} P(X = i \mid \lambda) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{\lambda^{i} e^{-\lambda}}{i!}$$

This is the **cumulative survival function** of the Poisson. If $P$ is very small (e.g., $< 10^{-5}$), the region is declared a peak.

**Step 4: Correct for Multiple Testing**
Apply Benjamini–Hochberg FDR correction:

$$q = \frac{P \times N}{R}$$

where $N$ is the total number of tested windows and $R$ is the rank of the p-value. Peaks with $q < 0.05$ are retained.

**Step 5: Worked Numerical Example**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU Exam Favorite):** Suppose at a candidate CTCF binding site, $\lambda = 2.0$ (expected background count) and we observe $k = 8$ tags. Compute the Poisson p-value.

First, compute $P(X < 8)$, i.e., the cumulative probability from $i = 0$ to $i = 7$:

$$P(X = 0) = \frac{2.0^{0} \cdot e^{-2.0}}{0!} = 1 \cdot 0.1353 = 0.1353$$

$$P(X = 1) = \frac{2.0^{1} \cdot e^{-2.0}}{1!} = 2.0 \cdot 0.1353 = 0.2707$$

$$P(X = 2) = \frac{2.0^{2} \cdot e^{-2.0}}{2!} = 4.0 \cdot 0.1353 \cdot 0.5 = 0.2707$$

$$P(X = 3) = \frac{2.0^{3} \cdot e^{-2.0}}{3!} = 8.0 \cdot 0.1353 \cdot 0.1667 = 0.1804$$

$$P(X = 4) = \frac{2.0^{4} \cdot e^{-2.0}}{4!} = 16.0 \cdot 0.1353 \cdot 0.04167 = 0.0902$$

$$P(X = 5) = \frac{2.0^{5} \cdot e^{-2.0}}{5!} = 32.0 \cdot 0.1353 \cdot 0.00833 = 0.0361$$

$$P(X = 6) = \frac{2.0^{6} \cdot e^{-2.0}}{6!} = 64.0 \cdot 0.1353 \cdot 0.001389 = 0.01203$$

$$P(X = 7) = \frac{2.0^{7} \cdot e^{-2.0}}{7!} = 128.0 \cdot 0.1353 \cdot 0.0001984 = 0.003437$$

Now sum these to get the cumulative probability:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{7} P(X = i) = 0.1353 + 0.2707 + 0.2707 + 0.1804 + 0.0902 + 0.0361 + 0.01203 + 0.003437$$

Let us add step by step:

$$0.1353 + 0.2707 = 0.4060$$

$$0.4060 + 0.2707 = 0.6767$$

$$0.6767 + 0.1804 = 0.8571$$

$$0.8571 + 0.0902 = 0.9473$$

$$0.9473 + 0.0361 = 0.9834$$

$$0.9834 + 0.01203 = 0.9954$$

$$0.9954 + 0.003437 = 0.9989$$

Therefore the survival function (p-value) is:

$$P(X \geq 8) = 1 - 0.9989 = 0.0011$$

**Interpretation:** Since $P = 0.0011 \ll 0.05$, the candidate region is **statistically significant** and is called a CTCF binding peak by MACS2.

### 3.2 Derivation: FRiP Score as a Quality Control Metric

The Fraction of Reads in Peaks (FRiP) measures how concentrated the sequencing reads are within called peaks. A higher FRiP means the library is genuinely enriched; a low FRiP indicates poor antibody specificity, low cell number, or excessive background noise.

**Step 1: Define the Numerator and Denominator**

$$FRiP = \frac{N_{peaks}}{N_{total}}$$

where $N_{peaks}$ is the number of uniquely mapped reads that overlap any called peak, and $N_{total}$ is the total number of uniquely mapped reads.

**Step 2: Apply ENCODE Quality Thresholds**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ENCODE Consortium Quality Standards (commonly tested in KTU exams):**
> - **High-quality ChIP-seq library:** $FRiP \geq 0.05$ for TFs, $FRiP \geq 0.20$ for histone marks.
> - **Acceptable ChIP-seq library:** $FRiP \geq 0.01$.
> - **High-quality ATAC-seq library:** $TSS_{score} \geq 7$ and $FRiP \geq 0.30$.

**Step 3: Worked Example**

A ChIP-seq experiment against H3K4me3 yields 25 million uniquely mapped reads. Peak calling (MACS2) identifies 80,000 peaks, and a total of 6.5 million reads fall inside these peaks. Compute the FRiP score.

$$FRiP = \frac{6.5 \times 10^{6}}{25 \times 10^{6}} = 0.26$$

Since $FRiP = 0.26 \geq 0.20$ (histone mark threshold), the library is classified as **high-quality**.

### 3.3 Derivation: TSS Enrichment for ATAC-seq Quality Control

The Transcription Start Site (TSS) enrichment score is the most widely accepted QC metric for ATAC-seq libraries. High-quality libraries should show a sharp, deep pile-up of reads at gene promoters.

**Step 1: Define a TSS Window**
Consider a window of $\pm 2$ kb around the TSS of every annotated gene. Aggregate the read coverage.

**Step 2: Compute the Enrichment Ratio**

$$TSS_{score} = \frac{\text{Mean read depth in TSS} \pm 2\text{kb windows}}{\text{Mean read depth in flanking regions (e.g., TSS} \pm 100\text{kb)}}$$

**Step 3: Apply Thresholds**
- $TSS_{score} \geq 7$: Excellent library.
- $4 \leq TSS_{score} < 7$: Acceptable.
- $TSS_{score} < 4$: Poor quality; library should be re-prepared.

**Step 4: Worked Example**
An ATAC-seq library has an average read depth of 8.2× at TSS $\pm$ 2 kb and an average of 1.1× in the flanking TSS $\pm$ 100 kb regions. Compute:

$$TSS_{score} = \frac{8.2}{1.1} = 7.45$$

This is a **high-quality** ATAC-seq library.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Analytical Parameter | ChIP-seq | ATAC-seq |
|----------------------|----------|----------|
| **Target** | Protein-bound DNA or specific histone marks | Open / accessible chromatin |
| **Enrichment chemistry** | Antibody-based immunoprecipitation | Tn5 transposase insertion |
| **Cell number required** | ~1–10 million cells | ~50,000 cells (bulk); 1 cell (scATAC-seq) |
| **Time to library** | 2–3 days | ~3–4 hours |
| **Sequencing depth** | 20–60 M reads | 30–100 M reads |
| **Fragment size signature** | Continuous 200–500 bp | Periodic 100, 200, 400 bp |
| **Peak types** | Sharp (TF) or Broad (histone) | Narrow, nucleosome-depleted |
| **Main QC metric** | FRiP $\geq 0.01$ | TSS enrichment $\geq 4$ |
| **Key tool** | MACS2 | MACS2 + Genrich / HMMRATAC |
| **Invention year / Origin** | 2007 (Johnson, Barski) | 2013 (Buenrostro) |

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*What is the difference between ChIP-seq and ATAC-seq in terms of their target biomolecules and enrichment chemistry?*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*Define FRiP score. State the minimum acceptable FRiP for a transcription factor ChIP-seq library as per ENCODE standards.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Fraction of Reads in Peaks (FRiP)** is the ratio of the number of uniquely mapped sequencing reads that fall within called peaks to the total number of uniquely mapped reads in the experiment.

$$FRiP = \frac{\text{Reads in peaks}}{\text{Total mapped reads}}$$

As per **ENCODE standards**, a *high-quality* TF ChIP-seq library should have $FRiP \geq 0.01$ (minimum acceptable), and histone mark libraries should have $FRiP \geq 0.20$. FRiP is a critical library QC metric, and values below 0.01 indicate failed or low-complexity libraries (3 marks).

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — ChIP-seq Focused** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) Describe in detail the wet-lab and computational workflow of ChIP-seq for identifying transcription factor binding sites. Include the role of formaldehyde, sonication, antibodies, and the MACS2 peak-calling algorithm.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The ChIP-seq workflow consists of **three experimental phases** and **two computational phases**.

**Phase 1 — In Vivo Crosslinking (1 mark):**
Viable cells are treated with **1% formaldehyde** for ~10 minutes at room temperature. Formaldehyde is a short-arm crosslinker (2 Å spacer) that covalently links primary amine groups on proteins to adenine/thymine bases on DNA. The reaction is quenched with 125 mM glycine to stop further crosslinking. This captures both stable and transient protein–DNA interactions *in situ*.

**Phase 2 — Chromatin Preparation and Sonication (1.5 marks):**
Cells are lysed in a buffer containing protease inhibitors (PMSF, aprotinin, leupeptin). Chromatin is extracted and sheared via high-frequency ultrasound to a target size of **200–500 bp**. Proper fragment size is critical: smaller fragments improve resolution but reduce specificity, while larger fragments decrease resolution.

**Phase 3 — Immunoprecipitation (1.5 marks):**
The sheared chromatin is incubated overnight with a **target-specific antibody** (e.g., anti-CTCF). The antibody–protein–DNA complex is captured using **Protein A or Protein G conjugated beads**. Stringent washes (low salt, high salt, LiCl, TE) remove non-specific binders. Crosslinks are then reversed by Proteinase K digestion at **65°C overnight**.

**Phase 4 — Library Preparation and Sequencing (1 mark):**
Purified DNA is end-repaired, dA-tailed, ligated to Illumina sequencing adapters, PCR-amplified, and sequenced using paired-end Illumina chemistry (2 × 50–150 bp). A matched **Input control** (non-immunoprecipitated chromatin) is sequenced in parallel to model background.

**Phase 5 — Computational Analysis with MACS2 (2 marks):**
Reads are aligned to the reference genome (GRCh38) using BWA or Bowtie2. PCR duplicates are removed. **MACS2** (Model-based Analysis for ChIP-Seq) calls peaks by:
1. Computing local background read density $\lambda$ in a sliding window.
2. Modeling tag counts under a Poisson distribution.
3. Computing $P(X \geq k \mid \lambda)$ for every candidate region.
4. Applying Benjamini–Hochberg FDR correction; peaks with $q < 0.05$ are retained.
Downstream tools such as HOMER annotate peaks to promoters, enhancers, and intergenic regions, and motif analysis identifies the cognate transcription factor binding motif.

**(b) Explain how the Poisson statistical model is used in MACS2 to identify significantly enriched peaks. Compute the p-value for a candidate peak where the local background lambda equals 3.0 and the observed tag count k equals 9.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Statistical Foundation (2 marks):**
MACS2 uses a **one-sided Poisson test** to determine whether the observed number of sequencing tags at a genomic window significantly exceeds the local background expectation. Under the null hypothesis, the count of reads in a window follows a Poisson distribution with rate parameter $\lambda$ equal to the local background density.

The Poisson probability mass function is:

$$P(X = i) = \frac{\lambda^{i} e^{-\lambda}}{i!}$$

The null hypothesis ($H_0$) is rejected when the observed count $k$ is significantly higher than $\lambda$. The p-value is the survival function:

$$P(X \geq k) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{\lambda^{i} e^{-\lambda}}{i!}$$

**Numerical Computation (5 marks):**
Given: $\lambda = 3.0$, $k = 9$. We must compute $\sum_{i=0}^{8} P(X = i)$ and subtract from 1.

The term $e^{-3.0} = 0.049787$.

$$\sum_{i=0}^{8} \frac{3.0^{i} e^{-3.0}}{i!} = e^{-3.0} \sum_{i=0}^{8} \frac{3.0^{i}}{i!}$$

Computing each term:

$$P(X = 0) = \frac{3.0^{0} \cdot 0.049787}{1} = 0.049787$$

$$P(X = 1) = \frac{3.0^{1} \cdot 0.049787}{1} = 0.149361$$

$$P(X = 2) = \frac{3.0^{2} \cdot 0.049787}{2} = \frac{9 \cdot 0.049787}{2} = 0.224042$$

$$P(X = 3) = \frac{3.0^{3} \cdot 0.049787}{6} = \frac{27 \cdot 0.049787}{6} = 0.224042$$

$$P(X = 4) = \frac{3.0^{4} \cdot 0.049787}{24} = \frac{81 \cdot 0.049787}{24} = 0.168031$$

$$P(X = 5) = \frac{3.0^{5} \cdot 0.049787}{120} = \frac{243 \cdot 0.049787}{120} = 0.100819$$

$$P(X = 6) = \frac{3.0^{6} \cdot 0.049787}{720} = \frac{729 \cdot 0.049787}{720} = 0.050409$$

$$P(X = 7) = \frac{3.0^{7} \cdot 0.049787}{5040} = \frac{2187 \cdot 0.049787}{5040} = 0.021604$$

$$P(X = 8) = \frac{3.0^{8} \cdot 0.049787}{40320} = \frac{6561 \cdot 0.049787}{40320} = 0.008101$$

Summing all nine terms:

$$0.049787 + 0.149361 + 0.224042 + 0.224042 + 0.168031 + 0.100819 + 0.050409 + 0.021604 + 0.008101$$

$$= 0.996196$$

Therefore:

$$P(X \geq 9) = 1 - 0.996196 = 0.003804$$

**Interpretation:** The computed p-value of **0.0038** is much smaller than the conventional significance threshold of $\alpha = 0.05$. The candidate region is therefore declared a statistically significant ChIP-seq peak, indicating a genuine transcription factor binding event.

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ATAC-seq Focused** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]

**(a) Describe the wet-lab and computational workflow of ATAC-seq. Explain the role of Tn5 transposase and discuss the biological significance of the characteristic fragment size distribution observed in ATAC-seq libraries.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Wet-Lab Workflow (3 marks):**
ATAC-seq begins with the collection of approximately **50,000 viable cells** (for bulk ATAC-seq). Cells are lysed in a cold, non-ionic detergent (e.g., NP-40 in ATAC-Resuspension Buffer) to isolate intact nuclei while minimizing mitochondrial contamination. The nuclei pellet is resuspended in the transposition reaction mix containing a **pre-assembled Tn5 transposase–adapter complex**, divalent cations (Mg²⁺), and a buffering system. The reaction is incubated at **37°C for 30–60 minutes**, during which Tn5 simultaneously fragments DNA and ligates sequencing adapters — a process called "**tagmentation**." The reaction is stopped with EDTA, and DNA is purified using silica columns (Qiagen MinElute). Limited-cycle PCR (5–12 cycles) with index primers adds Illumina-compatible barcodes, and bead-based size selection retains 100–600 bp fragments. The final library is QC-checked on a Bioanalyzer/TapeStation and quantified via Qubit before paired-end sequencing (2 × 50 bp is standard).

**Role of Tn5 Transposase (2 marks):**
Tn5 is a **cut-and-paste bacterial transposase** that has been engineered in the laboratory (Picelli et al., 2014) to function as a "molecular sandworm." When loaded with a pre-formed double-stranded adapter, Tn5 inserts the adapter directly into double-stranded DNA. Crucially, **Tn5 preferentially inserts into accessible, nucleosome-depleted DNA** because the bulky nucleosome structure physically blocks transposase binding. The catalytic mechanism requires two Tn5 monomers in a synaptic complex — each monomer nicks one DNA strand 9 bp apart, generating a 9-bp duplication of target sequence. This **9-bp offset** is the basis for computational Tn5-shift correction during peak calling (the `--shift -100 --extsize 200` parameter in MACS2 for ATAC-seq).

**Fragment Size Distribution (2 marks):**
After paired-end sequencing, the *insert size* (distance between read pairs) reveals a striking, periodic pattern:

- **Sub-nucleosomal fragments (~80–120 bp):** Derived from **nucleosome-depleted regions (NDRs)**, typically found at active promoters and enhancers where transcription factors are bound.
- **Mono-nucleosomal fragments (~180–250 bp):** Represent DNA wrapped around a single nucleosome (~147 bp core + linker).
- **Di-nucleosomal fragments (~300–400 bp):** Represent DNA wrapped around two adjacent nucleosomes.

The **presence and periodicity** of these peaks in a fragment size histogram is the single most important visual QC indicator of a successful ATAC-seq library. A high-quality library shows a clear **ladder pattern** dominated by the sub-nucleosomal and mono-nucleosomal peaks, reflecting genuine chromatin accessibility biology rather than random fragmentation.

**(b) What is the TSS enrichment score? Compute the TSS enrichment for an ATAC-seq library where the mean read depth in the TSS ± 2 kb window is 9.6× and the mean read depth in the flanking TSS ± 100 kb region is 1.4×. Comment on the library quality.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition of TSS Enrichment (2 marks):**
The **TSS Enrichment Score** is a quantitative quality-control metric for ATAC-seq libraries. It is calculated as the ratio of the average sequencing read coverage within a window of $\pm 2$ kb around annotated transcription start sites (TSS) to the average read coverage in the broader flanking regions (typically TSS $\pm 100$ kb). It measures how strongly the library has captured accessible chromatin at gene promoters, which is a defining feature of biologically meaningful ATAC-seq data.

$$TSS_{score} = \frac{\text{Mean read depth in TSS} \pm 2\text{ kb windows}}{\text{Mean read depth in TSS} \pm 100\text{ kb flanking regions}}$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**
Given: Mean depth at TSS $\pm 2$ kb = 9.6×, mean depth at flanking TSS $\pm 100$ kb = 1.4×.

$$TSS_{score} = \frac{9.6}{1.4} = 6.857$$

Rounded to two decimal places, the **TSS Enrichment Score is 6.86**.

**Interpretation and Library Quality Assessment (2 marks):**
According to ENCODE and standard ATAC-seq quality guidelines, a TSS enrichment score of $\geq 7$ indicates an **excellent** library, a score between 4 and 7 indicates an **acceptable** library, and a score below 4 indicates a **poor** library requiring re-preparation.

A score of **6.86** falls within the **4–7 range**, classifying this library as **acceptable but not optimal**. The strong promoter signal indicates that the Tn5 transposase has successfully accessed open chromatin at regulatory regions, but to upgrade the library to "excellent" status, the experimenter should:
1. Increase the cell number to 100,000 for higher complexity.
2. Optimize the lysis stringency to reduce mitochondrial DNA contamination.
3. Use a fresh, properly-titrated Tn5 lot, as aged transposase dramatically reduces tagmentation efficiency.

In summary, a TSS enrichment of 6.86 indicates a usable ATAC-seq library suitable for downstream peak calling and motif analysis, though borderline; investigators may consider improving the protocol for publication-quality data.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in ChIP-seq / ATAC-seq Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing ChIP-seq with RNA-seq** — ChIP-seq does *not* measure gene expression; it measures protein–DNA binding. Marks are lost when students treat ChIP-seq reads as "expression counts."
> 2. **Forgetting the Input control** — ChIP-seq without an Input control is statistically invalid. Always mention Input in your answer.
> 3. **Mixing up fragment size units** — Fragment sizes in ATAC-seq are in **base pairs (bp)**, not kilobases. The nucleosomal repeat is ~200 bp, not 200 kb.
> 4. **Skipping the Poisson p-value derivation steps** — Examiners expect explicit, term-by-term expansion of the Poisson summation. Writing the formula alone earns only 1–2 marks out of 7.
> 5. **Omitting the 9-bp Tn5 duplication** — When describing ATAC-seq chemistry, always mention the 9-bp target site duplication and the basis for the `--shift -100` MACS2 parameter.
> 6. **Confusing FRiP with TSS enrichment** — FRiP applies to ChIP-seq; TSS enrichment is the primary ATAC-seq metric. Do not interchange them.
> 7. **Using a vertical pipe in tables** — Marks may be deducted for broken table formatting due to misuse of `|` for absolute value.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **ChIP-seq** = Chromatin Immunoprecipitation + Sequencing → identifies **protein–DNA binding sites** and **histone modifications**.
- **ATAC-seq** = Assay for Transposase-Accessible Chromatin + sequencing → identifies **open / accessible chromatin regions**.
- ChIP-seq uses **1% formaldehyde crosslinking** + **antibody immunoprecipitation**.
- ATAC-seq uses a hyperactive **Tn5 transposase** for tagmentation.
- ChIP-seq requires **~1–10 million cells**; ATAC-seq requires only **~50,000 cells** (or single cells for scATAC-seq).
- **Sonication target** for ChIP-seq: **200–500 bp**.
- ATAC-seq **fragment size ladder**: ~100 bp (NDR), ~200 bp (mono-nuc), ~400 bp (di-nuc).
- **MACS2** is the dominant peak caller for both methods; it uses a **Poisson model** with parameter $\lambda$ for local background.
- **FRiP score** is the key ChIP-seq QC metric (≥ 0.01 for TF, ≥ 0.20 for histone marks).
- **TSS Enrichment** is the key ATAC-seq QC metric (≥ 4 acceptable, ≥ 7 excellent).
- Both methods require **paired-end sequencing** for accurate fragment size measurement.
- ChIP-seq peaks can be **sharp** (transcription factors) or **broad** (histone marks like H3K27me3).
- The **9-bp Tn5 target site duplication** is the molecular basis for ATAC-seq alignment shift correction.
- ENCODE consortium standards are the **industry benchmarks** for library quality and are frequently cited in KTU exam keys.
- Always include an **Input control** for ChIP-seq and report **mitochondrial read percentage** for ATAC-seq (should be < 20% in good libraries).
- **Reference genome**: GRCh38 (human) or mm10 (mouse) is the KTU 2024 standard.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT755 - NEXT GENERATION SEQUENCING TECHNOLOGIES: DATA ANALYSIS AND APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hi-C Sequencing - Molecular Principles and Approaches for Enrichment (Capture, Immunoprecipitation, DNA Crosslinking)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hi-C Sequencing** is a high-throughput chromosome conformation capture (3C) derivative technique that combines **chromatin immunoprecipitation (ChIP)** principles with **next-generation sequencing (NGS)** to map the three-dimensional (3D) spatial organization of genomes. It quantifies the frequency of physical interactions between genomic loci that are spatially proximal *in vivo* but may be linearly distant along the DNA molecule.

The foundational method was introduced by **Lieberman-Aiden et al. (2009)** in *Science*, establishing the principles of proximity ligation where crosslinked chromatin fragments are ligated *in situ* to create junction libraries that preserve spatial contact information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hi-C belongs to the family of "C" techniques (3C, 4C, 5C, Hi-C, Capture-C, ChIA-PET). All rely on the central dogma: **Crosslink → Cut → Ligate → Sequence**. Understanding this pipeline is critical for examination questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine reading a long encyclopedia (genome) where words are linearly arranged on pages, but you want to know which paragraphs on page 1 are *physically near* paragraphs on page 500 in the library. You cannot move the books. So you:

1. **Freeze the library** (formaldehyde crosslinking) — this glues together everything that is currently touching, including proteins and DNA.
2. **Tear it into small pieces** (restriction enzyme digestion) — keeping the glued contacts intact.
3. **Stitch the touching pieces together** (proximity ligation) — DNA ends that were spatially adjacent are now joined into single chimeric molecules.
4. **Sequence the junctions** (paired-end NGS) — every read pair tells you: "These two far-apart regions were touching in 3D space."

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Hi-C underpins our understanding of **topologically associating domains (TADs)**, **A/B compartments**, **chromatin loops**, and has applications in **cancer genomics** (structural variant detection), **developmental biology**, and **de novo genome assembly** (scaffolding).

## 1.3 Key Physical & Biochemical Constants

The following standard metrics govern Hi-C library preparation:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| Formaldehyde concentration | **1% (v/v)** | Crosslinking density |
| Crosslinking time | **10–15 minutes at room temperature** | Captures transient interactions |
| Restriction enzymes (4-cutter) | **MboI, DpnII, Sau3AI** (4-bp cutters) | High resolution |
| Restriction enzymes (6-cutter) | **HindIII, BamHI** (6-bp cutters) | Lower resolution, fewer fragments |
| Biotin-14-dATP fill-in | **37°C, 30–45 min** | End-labeling for capture |
| Ligation temperature | **16°C overnight (12–16 hours)** | Promotes intramolecular ligation |
| Fragment size selection | **300–500 bp** | Optimal for Illumina sequencing |
| Sequencing read type | **Paired-end, 2 × 50–150 bp** | Junctional read orientation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Molecular Pipeline of Hi-C

### Stage 1: DNA-Protein Crosslinking (The "Freeze" Step)

**Molecular Principle:** Formaldehyde (HCHO) is a short-arm (≈2 Å) bifunctional crosslinker that forms covalent methylene bridges (–CH₂–) between:
- Primary amines of lysine/arginine residues on **histones and chromatin-binding proteins**
- Exocyclic amines of **adenine, cytosine, and guanine** bases on DNA

$$\text{Protein-NH}_2 + \text{HCHO} \rightarrow \text{Protein-NH-CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{DNA-NH}_2 \rightarrow \text{Protein-NH-CH}_2\text{-NH-DNA}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why formaldehyde?** Its short crosslinking arm (~2 Å) ensures only **direct physical contacts** are captured, minimizing background noise from random collisions. Longer crosslinkers (e.g., disuccinimidyl glutarate, DSG) capture indirect interactions but increase noise.

### Stage 2: Chromatin Digestion (The "Cut" Step)

A **restriction endonuclease** cleaves crosslinked chromatin. The choice of enzyme sets the **resolution ceiling** of the experiment.

The theoretical maximum resolution is governed by:

$$R_{\text{resolution}} = \frac{L_{\text{genome}}}{N_{\text{fragments}} \times N_{\text{coverage}}}$$

For a 4-cutter like MboI on the human genome (3 × 10⁹ bp):

$$N_{\text{fragments}} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{4^4} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{256} \approx 1.17 \times 10^7 \text{ fragments}$$

For a 6-cutter like HindIII:

$$N_{\text{fragments}} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{4^6} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{4096} \approx 7.3 \times 10^5 \text{ fragments}$$

**4-cutter = ~16× higher resolution** than 6-cutters, but generates more data and demands deeper sequencing.

### Stage 3: Biotin Labeling & Proximity Ligation

Sticky DNA ends are filled in using **DNA polymerase I Klenow fragment** with biotin-labeled nucleotides (biotin-14-dATP, plus dCTP, dGTP, dTTP). This marks all ligation junctions.

**Ligation conditions are tuned to favor intramolecular (within-complex) ligation over intermolecular events:**

$$\text{Concentration}_{\text{DNA}} \approx 1\text{–}3 \text{ ng/μL (very dilute)}$$

The probability of two DNA ends ligating depends on their spatial proximity in 3D space, which is precisely what Hi-C measures.

### Stage 4: Enrichment — Capture, Immunoprecipitation, and Crosslink Reversal

This is the **central theme** of our KTU module. There are three principal enrichment strategies:

#### A. Capture Approach (Streptavidin-Based Affinity Purification)
- Biotinylated ligation junctions are pulled down using **streptavidin-coated magnetic beads**.
- Streptavidin binds biotin with extraordinary affinity ($K_d \approx 10^{-14}$ M), one of the strongest non-covalent biological interactions.
- This step **purifies only chimeric (junction) fragments** from the total ligation mixture, dramatically enriching the Hi-C library.

#### B. Immunoprecipitation (ChIP-Based Enrichment)
- For **ChIA-PET** or **Hi-ChIP** variants, antibodies against specific proteins (e.g., CTCF, RNAPII, cohesin) are used to **immunoprecipitate protein-mediated interactions**.
- The protein of interest must be **crosslinked to DNA** in Stage 1.
- This adds a layer of biological specificity: only interactions mediated by that protein are captured.

#### C. Crosslink Reversal
- After pull-down, samples are treated with **proteinase K** and **heat (65°C overnight)** to reverse formaldehyde crosslinks, releasing pure DNA for library construction.

### Stage 5: Paired-End Sequencing & Computational Reconstruction

Sequenced read pairs are mapped to a reference genome. The **insert size distribution** is diagnostic: Hi-C junctions show an *enrichment* of paired-end reads mapping to **distant genomic regions** that are nonetheless spatially proximal.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|--------|---------------------|-------------|-------|
| $L_g$ | $L_g$ | Genome length | bp |
| $N_f$ | $N_f = \frac{L_g}{4^n}$ | Number of restriction fragments (n = cut-site length) | dimensionless |
| $R$ | $R = \frac{L_g}{N_f \times C}$ | Theoretical Hi-C resolution | bp |
| $C$ | $C$ | Sequencing coverage per fragment | dimensionless |
| $K_d$ | $K_d \approx 10^{-14}$ M | Streptavidin-biotin affinity constant | M (molar) |
| $T_m$ | $T_m$ | Melting/reversal temperature of crosslinks | °C |
| $\eta$ | $\eta = \frac{\text{Valid Pairs}}{\text{Total Pairs}}$ | Library quality metric (≥ 30% ideal) | % |
| $P_{\text{inter/intra}}$ | $\frac{P_{\text{inter}}}{P_{\text{intra}}}$ | cis-to-trans ratio (diagnostic of Hi-C quality) | ratio |
| $\bar{d}$ | $\bar{d}$ | Mean genomic distance of contacts | bp / Mb |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The $R$ formula directly determines sequencing depth requirements. A 1-kb resolution Hi-C experiment on the human genome requires **>1 billion read pairs**, which is a key budget/planning parameter in genomics pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretically Required Sequencing Depth for a Given Resolution

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the minimum number of read pairs required to achieve 1-kb resolution in a Hi-C experiment on the human genome using MboI (a 4-cutter), assuming a binomial contact model with a per-fragment capture probability $p = 0.05$.

**Step 1: Calculate the number of restriction fragments**

$$N_f = \frac{L_g}{4^n} = \frac{3 \times 10^9 \text{ bp}}{4^4 \text{ bp}} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{256}$$

$$N_f = 1.171875 \times 10^7 \approx 1.17 \times 10^7 \text{ fragments}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the target coverage per fragment**

The target resolution $R = 1000$ bp means each 1-kb window must be sequenced to a depth sufficient for reliable contact calling. The number of 1-kb windows is:

$$N_w = \frac{L_g}{R} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{10^3} = 3 \times 10^6 \text{ windows}$$

**Step 3: Apply the capture probability model**

The probability that a given restriction fragment is captured at least once follows a binomial distribution. To achieve an average of $C$ contacts per fragment, the total number of ligation events $N_{\text{ligation}}$ must satisfy:

$$N_{\text{ligation}} = \frac{N_f \times C}{p}$$

For a reasonable contact coverage of $C = 100$ per fragment:

$$N_{\text{ligation}} = \frac{1.17 \times 10^7 \times 100}{0.05} = \frac{1.17 \times 10^9}{0.05} = 2.34 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 4: Account for valid pair fraction**

A typical Hi-C library has a **valid pair fraction** $\eta = 0.30$ (only ~30% of read pairs represent valid ligation junctions; the rest are unligated, self-circles, or dangling ends).

$$N_{\text{reads}} = \frac{N_{\text{ligation}}}{\eta} = \frac{2.34 \times 10^{10}}{0.30} = 7.8 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 5: Final Answer**

$$\boxed{N_{\text{reads}} \approx 7.8 \times 10^{10} \text{ paired-end reads}}$$

This corresponds to ~**780 gigabases** of sequencing — a typical deep Hi-C experiment on the human genome. For KTU problems, this calculation demonstrates the **massive depth** required for high-resolution chromatin mapping.

## 3.2 Derivation: Streptavidin-Biotin Binding Capacity

**Problem:** Calculate the moles of biotinylated DNA retained by 100 μL of streptavidin magnetic beads (binding capacity 6500 pmol biotin/mL of bead slurry).

**Step 1: Convert volume to binding capacity**

$$B = 100 \text{ μL} \times \frac{6500 \text{ pmol}}{\text{mL}} = 100 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mL} \times 6500 \text{ pmol/mL}$$

$$B = 0.1 \times 6500 = 650 \text{ pmol of biotin} = 6.5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2: Convert to DNA mass (assume average fragment = 400 bp, MW ≈ 264 g/mol per bp)**

$$M_{\text{fragment}} = 400 \text{ bp} \times 660 \text{ g/(mol·bp)} = 2.64 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\text{Mass of DNA} = n \times M = 6.5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ mol} \times 2.64 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\text{Mass of DNA} = 1.716 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g} = \boxed{171.6 \text{ μg}}$$

This is the theoretical maximum DNA captured — a critical figure when designing Hi-C library input amounts.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Enrichment Strategies

| Feature | Streptavidin Capture | Immunoprecipitation (ChIP) | Hybrid (Hi-ChIP) |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------------|------------------|
| **Specificity** | All ligation junctions | Protein-mediated only | Protein-mediated |
| **Required input cells** | 2–5 million | 10–20 million | ~1 million (low-input) |
| **Number of steps** | Moderate | High (2 IP rounds possible) | Reduced |
| **Background noise** | Low | Moderate | Low |
| **Resolution** | Highest | Moderate | Moderate-High |
| **Cost per sample** | High (beads) | High (antibodies) | Lower |
| **Typical use case** | Whole-genome 3D mapping | CTCF/RNAP2 loops | Single-cell or low-input applications |
| **KTU weightage** | High | High | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU exams:** The transition from traditional Hi-C to **Hi-ChIP** and **CUT&Tag-based 3C** methods represents a major engineering optimization, reducing input requirements by an order of magnitude.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]
**Q1.** Define Hi-C sequencing. What is the role of formaldehyde in the Hi-C protocol?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Hi-C is a chromosome conformation capture technique combined with next-generation sequencing used to study the three-dimensional organization of genomes in the nucleus. It maps physical interactions between genomic loci that are spatially proximal but may be linearly distant.

**Role of formaldehyde (1 mark each):**
- Formaldehyde (1% v/v) is a bifunctional crosslinker that forms covalent methylene bridges (–CH₂–) between primary amines of chromatin-bound proteins (histones, transcription factors) and DNA bases.
- This **freezes** all existing physical contacts in the nucleus, preserving them through subsequent biochemical processing.
- Its short crosslinking arm (~2 Å) ensures only **direct physical contacts** are captured, reducing false-positive background.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**Q2.** Differentiate between streptavidin-biotin capture and immunoprecipitation as enrichment strategies in Hi-C.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Streptavidin-Biotin Capture | Immunoprecipitation |
|--------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Pulls down biotinylated ligation junctions using streptavidin beads | Pulls down protein-DNA complexes using specific antibodies |
| **Specificity** | Captures all ligation junctions (genome-wide) | Captures only protein-mediated interactions |
| **Input requirement** | 2–5 million cells | 10–20 million cells (higher) |
| **Example method** | Standard Hi-C | ChIA-PET, Hi-ChIP |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model]

**Q.A.** **(a)** Describe in detail the molecular principles underlying Hi-C sequencing. Explain the rationale behind each step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat labeled schematic, explain the role of **DNA crosslinking** in Hi-C. Discuss why formaldehyde is preferred over other crosslinkers. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**1. Definition & Rationale (2 marks):**
Hi-C is a proximity-ligation technique that converts 3D spatial proximity of chromatin into sequenceable ligation products. The central hypothesis is that ligation frequency between two loci reflects their 3D spatial proximity in the nucleus.

**2. Step-by-Step Molecular Principle (5 marks):**

- **Crosslinking:** Formaldehyde forms –CH₂– bridges between proteins and DNA, locking transient chromatin contacts.
- **Digestion:** A 4-cutter restriction enzyme (e.g., MboI) fragments chromatin at ~256 bp intervals, generating manageable pieces while preserving crosslinks.
- **Biotinylation:** Klenow polymerase fills in 5′ overhangs with biotin-14-dATP, tagging every restriction site end.
- **Ligation:** Under dilute conditions, DNA ends within the same crosslinked complex ligate preferentially, creating chimeric molecules encoding 3D contacts.
- **Capture:** Streptavidin beads selectively enrich biotinylated junctions.
- **Sequencing:** Paired-end reads map to a reference genome; ligation junctions reveal contacts.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**1. Schematic (4 marks):**

The student should draw a labeled diagram showing:
- Nuclear compartment with chromatin fibers
- Formaldehyde molecules forming bridges
- Histone octamer–DNA contact points
- Crosslinked protein-DNA adducts
- Indication that bridges are ~2 Å in length

**2. Why Formaldehyde (3 marks):**
- **Short crosslinking arm (2 Å):** Captures only direct, high-confidence contacts.
- **Reversible crosslinks:** Can be reversed by heat (65°C) and proteinase K, allowing recovery of pure DNA.
- **Cell permeable:** Penetrates intact cells and tissues rapidly.
- **Membrane permeable, nuclear permeable:** Crosslinks happen *in vivo*, preserving native architecture.
- **Compared to DSG (longer arm, ~7.7 Å):** DSG captures more indirect interactions but with higher background noise; thus it is not preferred for high-resolution Hi-C.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model — Alternative Choice]

**Q.B.** **(a)** Explain the principles and procedure of **streptavidin-biotin capture** in Hi-C library preparation. Include the binding kinetics and its effect on library yield. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **chromatin immunoprecipitation (ChIP)** as an enrichment strategy in 3C-based methods. Compare ChIA-PET and Hi-ChIP in terms of workflow and data output. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**1. Principle of Streptavidin-Biotin Capture (3 marks):**
- Biotin (vitamin B7) is a small molecule covalently attached to the 5′ end of dATP (as biotin-14-dATP) during the fill-in step.
- **Streptavidin** is a tetrameric protein from *Streptomyces avidinii* with four biotin-binding sites.
- Binding affinity: $K_d \approx 10^{-14}$ M — among the strongest non-covalent interactions in biology.
- This exceptional affinity makes the pull-down nearly quantitative.

**2. Procedure (2 marks):**
- Biotinylated DNA is mixed with streptavidin-coated magnetic beads.
- Beads are washed stringently to remove non-biotinylated DNA.
- Captured DNA is eluted and processed for sequencing.

**3. Effect on Library Yield (2 marks):**
- Typical capture efficiency: 70–90% of biotinylated DNA is recovered.
- This step contributes the major enrichment of valid ligation junctions.
- A successful capture should yield a **valid pair fraction (η) ≥ 30%**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**1. ChIP in 3C Methods (3 marks):**
- After crosslinking, chromatin is sonicated or enzymatically fragmented.
- An **antibody** specific to a protein of interest (CTCF, RNAPII, SMC1, etc.) is added.
- Antibody–protein–DNA complexes are pulled down using **Protein A/G beads**.
- After reversal, only DNA fragments bound by the target protein are sequenced, providing protein-specific contact maps.

**2. ChIA-PET vs Hi-ChIP Comparison (4 marks):**

| Feature | ChIA-PET | Hi-ChIP |
|---------|----------|---------|
| **Workflow** | Long (5–6 days); in-solution ligation | Short (2 days); on-bead ligation |
| **Input cells** | 50–100 million | 1–5 million |
| **Ligation method** | PET linker-mediated | In situ on bead |
| **Specificity** | High (2 IP rounds possible) | High (single IP) |
| **Cost** | High | Lower |
| **Data complexity** | Higher (PET tags add complexity) | Simpler |
| **Application** | Deep protein-specific interactome | Routine protein-centric 3D mapping |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often confuse **streptavidin-biotin capture** (enriches *all* junctions) with **immunoprecipitation** (enriches *protein-specific* interactions). Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this.
> - Forgetting to mention the **valid pair fraction (η ≥ 30%)** as a QC metric is a frequent error in 14-mark answers.
> - Confusing **formaldehyde crosslinking** (short arm, reversible) with **DSG** (long arm, irreversible without hydroxylamine) is a known trap.
> - Always state the **K_d of streptavidin-biotin interaction** (10⁻¹⁴ M) in capture-based questions — it is a high-yield KTU 2024 exam fact.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 — Hi-C Essentials (Save this for last-minute revision)**

- **Hi-C** = 3C + NGS = proximity ligation + paired-end sequencing
- **Five stages:** Crosslink → Cut → Label (biotin) → Ligate → Capture → Sequence
- **Formaldehyde (1%, ~2 Å arm):** Short, reversible, direct contact capture
- **Restriction enzymes:** 4-cutters (MboI) = high resolution; 6-cutters (HindIII) = low resolution
- **Biotin-14-dATP:** Labels all restriction sites; pulled down by streptavidin
- **Streptavidin-biotin K_d = 10⁻¹⁴ M** (strongest non-covalent bond in nature)
- **Ligation conditions:** Dilute, 16°C, 12–16 hr (favors intramolecular events)
- **Three enrichment strategies:**
  1. **Capture** (streptavidin-biotin) — all junctions
  2. **Immunoprecipitation** (ChIP-based) — protein-specific
  3. **Hybrid Hi-ChIP** — low-input, on-bead ligation
- **Quality metric:** Valid pair fraction η ≥ 30%
- **Resolution formula:** $R = \frac{L_g}{4^n \times C}$ where n = cut-site length
- **Cis-to-trans ratio:** Diagnostic of Hi-C library quality (high cis = good)
- **Key read-out:** TADs, A/B compartments, chromatin loops
- **Applications:** Genome scaffolding, cancer SVs, developmental biology, gene regulation
